summary_metadata.summary: Megan, Raj, and Brian convened on day three of an ongoing outage affecting the Detect product's event processing pipeline. Raj explained that a single point of failure in the event ingestion layer caused a cascading failure, leaving customers with zero threat monitoring visibility for approximately six hours. The remediation plan includes deploying redundant active-active processing nodes (live by end of day) and implementing a circuit breaker pattern (by Wednesday evening), with full tenant visibility restored in a phased rollout by tomorrow morning. The team aligned on communication protocols, retroactive security analysis, and a postmortem timeline.

action_items.text: Draft updated customer communication explaining the phased rollout and revised timeline within the hour
Send evening status update to Megan and Brian once the first phase of the redundant node rollout is complete
Deliver internal retroactive event analysis summary report by Wednesday
Prepare affected account breakdown with priority tiers and impact durations for the postmortem by Thursday

key_moments.text: Brian reveals 112 open support tickets tied to the outage, with ~30 from priority 1 or 2 accounts and several vocal customers
key_moments.speaker: Brian Cho

key_moments.text: Raj confirms customers had zero threat monitoring data for six hours, with events queuing but not processing
key_moments.speaker: Raj Kapoor

key_moments.text: Megan raises the critical concern that no affirmative statements can be made to customers about security posture during the outage window
key_moments.speaker: Megan Lawson

key_moments.text: Raj pushes back on compressing the timeline, stating he would rather give a conservative estimate and hit it given the existing credibility issue
key_moments.speaker: Raj Kapoor

turns.text: Alright, I think we're all on — Raj, Brian, can you both hear me okay?
Yeah, I'm here. Audio's good.
Yep, I'm on. Give me one sec, I'm just pulling up the incident doc.

Sure, take your time. So, uh, the reason I wanted to get us together today — obviously we're still in the middle of this, day three of the outage window — I want to make sure we have a clear remediation plan and that we're all aligned before we send anything else externally.
Yeah, agreed. I've been heads-down with the engineering team since Saturday and I think we have a pretty solid picture of the root cause at this point. Do you want me to just walk through it?
Please, yeah. Go ahead.

Okay so — and Brian, let me know if the technical depth here is useful for you or if you want me to translate as I go — basically what we're looking at is a cascading failure in the event processing pipeline. The short version is we had a single point of failure in the event ingestion layer, and when that node went down, there was no fallback. So events just started backing up and then the whole downstream monitoring stack lost visibility.
Yeah no that's — that's helpful context actually. My team has been getting hammered with tickets since Saturday morning and I want to make sure I understand what I'm telling customers, so.
How many tickets are we at now, roughly?

As of this morning we're at a hundred and twelve open cases specifically tied to the Detect outage. About thirty of those are priority one or two accounts. We've got a handful of customers that are, uh, pretty vocal about it.
Okay. That's — yeah, that tracks with what I've been seeing from the account side. Raj, continuing on the root cause — what's the fix look like?

So the fix is two-pronged. The immediate thing we're doing — and this is already in progress, the team started on it yesterday — is deploying redundant processing nodes. So instead of relying on a single ingestion point, we're moving to an active-active configuration. That should eliminate the single point of failure entirely.
And the second piece?

The second piece is implementing a circuit breaker pattern on the pipeline. So essentially, if one component starts degrading, the circuit breaker trips before it can take down the whole chain. It's a more resilient architecture overall. We should have had this from the start, honestly, but — yeah.

I appreciate you saying that. I think — and I want to be candid here — I think there's going to be a postmortem conversation about how this got built the way it did, but I want to table that for now and focus on the remediation. What's the timeline looking like for full restoration?

Current estimate is we'll have the redundant nodes live by end of day today. The circuit breaker implementation is a little more involved — that's probably forty-eight hours from now, so, Wednesday evening at the earliest. But the monitoring visibility issue, the core thing customers are experiencing, that should be resolved today.
Okay so when you say resolved today — are we talking full visibility restored across all tenants, or is it phased?
It'll be phased. We're going to roll it out starting with, uh, our larger enterprise tenants first and then work down. Realistically all tenants should have visibility restored by tomorrow morning.
Okay. That's a little frustrating because I've been telling customers we're targeting end of today. Can we — is there any way to compress that timeline or is tomorrow morning pretty firm?
Brian, I get it, but I'd rather give customers a conservative timeline and hit it than promise end of day and miss. We've already got a credibility issue here.
Yeah, no, that's fair. That's fair. I just — I need to go back and update a lot of those tickets and some of these customers are going to push back.

I can help draft the updated customer communication if that's useful, Brian. I think we should have something that acknowledges the delay, explains the phased rollout without getting too deep in the weeds technically, and gives them the concrete timeline.
Yeah, that would actually be really helpful. My team is stretched pretty thin right now.
Raj, on the six hours of no visibility — I want to make sure I understand this correctly. During that window, customers had zero threat monitoring data? Like nothing was coming through?

Correct. Events were being ingested at the edge but they were just... queuing up and not processing. So from a customer-facing dashboard perspective, it looked like nothing was happening. No alerts, no detections, nothing. Which is — yeah, that's bad.
And we're confident that there were no actual threats during that window that went undetected? Or is that — can we even say that?

That's a really important question and the honest answer is we've been doing retroactive processing of the queued events and so far we haven't identified any threats that were active during that window, but I want to be careful about saying definitively that nothing happened. We're still working through the backlog.

Okay so we cannot make any affirmative statements to customers about security posture during that window. Got it. Brian, make sure your team is not — if anyone's asking that question, the answer is we're doing a full retroactive analysis and we'll communicate findings.
Understood. Yeah, I'll make sure that's clear in the team briefing. We've had a few customers ask exactly that and I've been kind of hedging, so it's good to have a cleaner answer.
Raj, what's the ETA on completing that retroactive processing? I want to know when we can close the loop on that.

We're probably looking at another twenty-four hours to fully process and review the backlog. I'll have a summary report by Wednesday that we can share internally, and then we can figure out what we communicate to customers from there.

Alright. Let's talk about the postmortem briefly — not to get into the full discussion, but I want to align on the process. Are we thinking internal postmortem first and then a customer-facing summary, or are we doing those in parallel?

I'd want to do the internal one first. We need to have our story straight before we put anything external out. I'm thinking we do a full engineering postmortem by end of this week, and then next week we can work on a customer-facing incident report.
That timeline works for me. Brian, from a support perspective, is there anything you need for the postmortem process? Like data from the ticket queue or customer impact analysis?

Yeah, I can pull together a breakdown of affected accounts, priority tiers, how long each was impacted — that kind of thing. That should be useful for the postmortem. I'll have that ready by Thursday.

Perfect. Okay, so just to summarize where we are — redundant nodes going live today, circuit breaker implementation by Wednesday, full tenant visibility restored by tomorrow morning, retroactive event analysis complete by Wednesday, internal postmortem end of this week, customer-facing incident report next week. Does that capture it?
That's accurate from my end.
Yeah, that matches what I've got. I'll start updating tickets this afternoon once Megan sends over draft language.
Great. I'll get that to you within the hour, Brian. Raj — anything else you need from me or from the product side to unblock the engineering work?
Not right now. The team knows what they're doing. I'll send you both a status update this evening once the first phase of the rollout is done.
Sounds good. Thanks both. Let's talk again Wednesday to review where we are on the circuit breaker and the retroactive analysis. I'll send a hold.
Works for me.
Yeah, talk Wednesday. Thanks everyone.

meeting_metadata.title: Support Case #9279 - Summit Trust Billing Inquiry

summary_metadata.summary: Gregory Fisk, IT Director at Summit Trust, called regarding a billing discrepancy on his January invoice showing 1,400 seats for Aegis Identity when their contract specifies 1,200 seats. Sarah Chen investigated and traced the overage to approximately 200 test accounts created during a December migration that were never deprovisioned. Sarah agreed to submit a billing adjustment request to the finance team, which will take 5-7 business days to review. Gregory also received guidance on bulk deprovisioning test accounts and setting up usage threshold alerts to prevent recurrence.

action_items.text: Submit billing adjustment request to finance team for the 200-seat overage on invoice INV-2026-00847 and send reference number to Gregory
Email Gregory the knowledge base article on bulk deprovisioning and include steps for configuring usage threshold alerts
Have IT team bulk deprovision the ~200 test accounts created during the December migration before the February billing period closes
Configure usage threshold alerts in the admin console under Account Settings > Billing and Usage

key_moments.text: Gregory identifies a 200-seat discrepancy on his January invoice versus his contracted 1,200-seat count
key_moments.speaker: Gregory Fisk

key_moments.text: Root cause identified: ~200 undeprovisioned test accounts from December migration are inflating the billed seat count
key_moments.speaker: Sarah Chen

key_moments.text: Gregory acknowledges partial responsibility for the oversight and shifts focus constructively to resolving the invoice
key_moments.speaker: Gregory Fisk

key_moments.text: Gregory requests proactive seat-count threshold alerts to avoid discovering overages on future invoices
key_moments.speaker: Gregory Fisk

turns.text: Thank you for calling Aegis Cloud Security support, this is Sarah Chen, how can I help you today?
Hi Sarah, yeah this is Gregory Fisk, I'm the IT Director over at Summit Trust, I have a support case open — case number 9279 — regarding a billing question.
Great, let me pull that up right now, Gregory — and thanks for the case number, that helps a lot.
Sure, no problem.
Okay, I'm seeing case 9279 here, Summit Trust, billing inquiry submitted... looks like February 4th. So can you walk me through what you're seeing on your end?

Yeah so, uh, we received our January invoice last week and there's a line item on there that doesn't quite match what we expected based on our contract. It's showing a charge for — let me pull this up — okay, it's showing a charge for 1,400 seats under Aegis Identity, but we only have 1,200 licensed users.
Okay, so a discrepancy of 200 seats on the Aegis Identity module. Got it. And just to confirm — was this your January invoice, so the billing period would have been January 1st through January 31st?
That's correct, yeah, January billing period.
Alright. And do you have the invoice number handy, or should I just work from the account?
Um, give me just a second... yeah, it's INV-2026-00847.

Perfect, INV-2026-00847, let me bring that up... okay, I can see the invoice. And yeah, I can see the Identity module line item — 1,400 seats at the per-seat rate. Let me cross-reference this against your contract terms and your provisioned seat count.
Right, and I mean, our contract was renewed in October, I believe, and at that time we explicitly set the seat count at 1,200. So I'm not sure where the 1,400 is coming from.

Yeah, that's a fair question. Let me look at the contract record... okay so I'm seeing the renewal executed October 14th, 2025, and the contracted seat count for Aegis Identity is listed as 1,200 seats. So you're correct there.
Right, so where's the 1,400 coming from? Is there some kind of overage charge? Because we weren't notified of any overage.

That's actually a good point and let me dig into the usage data for January. Our billing system will sometimes pick up peak concurrent provisioned users during the billing cycle, and if that number exceeded your contracted seat count at any point, it can trigger overage billing depending on how your contract is structured.
Hmm. I wasn't aware that was how it worked. We haven't had 1,400 active users — I mean our headcount isn't even close to that.

Understood, and that's worth looking at more closely. So I'm pulling up the provisioning logs for your account for January... okay so what I'm seeing here is interesting — there were 1,400 user accounts in a provisioned state within the Aegis Identity system during January, but let me look at whether those were all active or if some were in, like, a pending or deprovisioned queue.

Yeah, we did do a migration in December where we were onboarding users from a legacy system, and I know there were some test accounts created during that process. Could those still be showing as provisioned?

That could very well explain it. If test accounts or staging accounts were created and not explicitly deprovisioned, the system would count those against your seat total. Do you know approximately how many test accounts were created during that migration?
I'd have to check with my team but, uh, off the top of my head I'm thinking maybe 150 to 200 accounts, something in that range. We were testing SSO integrations with a few internal apps.

Okay, so that does line up with the discrepancy — 1,200 contracted plus roughly 200 test accounts would get you to the 1,400 that was billed. The system doesn't differentiate between test and production users unless they're tagged and excluded explicitly, which is something we'd typically walk customers through during a migration engagement.

Right, well, that's — I mean, that's a process thing we probably should have caught on our end too, so I'm not going to sit here and say it's entirely on Aegis. But the question now is what do we do about the invoice.

Absolutely, and that's a reasonable question. So what I can do from a support side is document the root cause here — essentially the overage was driven by non-production test accounts that weren't deprovisioned post-migration — and submit a billing adjustment request to our finance team. That goes to them with my notes and they review it.
Okay. And what's the typical turnaround on something like that?

Usually 5 to 7 business days for the review, and then if a credit or adjustment is approved it would appear on your next billing cycle. I can't guarantee the outcome of the review, but given that the overage appears to be tied to test accounts from a migration, it's a scenario our billing team has handled before.

That's fine. I'd appreciate you submitting that. And — sorry, one more thing — those test accounts, I need to get those cleaned up regardless. Is there a way to bulk deprovision them, or does someone have to go in and do it one by one?

Yeah, there's a bulk deprovision option in the Aegis Identity admin console — if you go to User Management and then Provisioning, you can filter by account status, creation date, and there's also a tag-based filter if any of those test accounts were tagged during setup. You can select multiple accounts and deprovision them in one action.
Okay good, that's helpful. I'll have my team take care of that this week. We definitely don't want to have the same issue on the February invoice.

That makes sense, and I'd actually recommend doing it sooner rather than later so that the February billing period doesn't pick up the elevated seat count again. If you want, I can send you a quick knowledge base article that walks through the bulk deprovision steps — sometimes it's easier to have that written out.
Yeah, that would be useful, if you could send that to the email on the case that'd be great.
Will do. I'll send that along with a summary of what we discussed today and the billing adjustment request reference number once I've submitted it. Should all come through within the next hour or so.

Perfect. And just — out of curiosity — going forward, is there a way to get some kind of alert or notification if our seat count gets close to the contracted limit? Because I'd rather know proactively than find out on an invoice.

That's actually a really good question. There are usage threshold alerts you can configure in the admin console — under Account Settings, Billing and Usage, you can set a threshold, say 90% of your contracted seat count, and you'll get an email notification when you hit it. That's probably worth setting up.
Yeah, I'll definitely look into that. I don't think we have that configured right now.
I'll include a note about that in the follow-up email as well, just so you have the steps. Is there anything else I can help you with on this call today?
No, I think that covers it. I appreciate you walking through all of this — it's helpful to understand what actually happened rather than just having a number on a bill that doesn't make sense.

Of course, that's what we're here for. I'll get everything documented and submitted, and you should hear back from the billing team within that 5 to 7 business day window. If you have any other questions in the meantime, feel free to reply to the case email and it'll come back to me.
Sounds good. Thanks Sarah.
Thank you Gregory, have a good rest of your day.

summary_metadata.summary: The engineering team held a sprint status check-in covering the Detect pipeline reliability work and the upcoming Comply v2 launch. Mike Romano reported being 60% through the event ingestion refactor, with the end-of-March deadline assessed at roughly 60-40 odds due to uncertainty around Kafka configuration and redundant node setup. Tyler Washington completed alert deduplication work and will assist by spiking on the Kafka investigation to help reduce risk. Tom Bradley will communicate to product that the March target stands but carries acknowledged technical uncertainty, while Chris Lee continues weekly syncs with Priya's team on the Comply v2 April launch.

action_items.text: Carve out time this week to investigate and document Kafka configuration interactions with the redundant node setup to provide clearer inputs for Mike
Finalize and clean up the retry logic PR, targeting ready-for-review status by Wednesday
Review Mike's retry logic PR on Thursday and update the sprint board to reflect Tyler's merged deduplication tickets
Communicate to product that end-of-March is still the target for Detect reliability work but flag active technical uncertainty being worked down

summary_metadata.summary: Daniel Okafor and solutions architect Kevin O'Brien met with Thomas Hargrove of Redwood to introduce Comply v2 and discuss support for their ISO 27001 certification journey. Thomas shared that Redwood is in early stages of ISO prep, with a formal audit targeted for Q1 2027, and highlighted pain points around evidence collection and continuous monitoring. Kevin walked through Comply v2's on-demand reporting engine, which maps live environment data to Annex A controls and supports multi-framework coverage including HIPAA and SOC 2. The team agreed on a clear set of next steps: activating Comply v2 on Redwood's tenant, running a baseline report, and scheduling a technical working session with Thomas and his security analyst Priya.

action_items.text: Send calendar invite for technical working session in two weeks, including Thomas and Priya
Activate Comply v2 module on Redwood's tenant and complete configuration review
Send prep document explaining what to expect from the baseline ISO 27001 readiness report
Have Priya prepared to join the technical working session after baseline report is run

key_moments.text: Thomas reveals his CISO has been pressuring him about ISO 27001 since January, establishing urgency for the engagement
key_moments.speaker: Thomas Hargrove

key_moments.text: Kevin demonstrates that Comply v2 maps live environment data to specific ISO 27001 Annex A controls, generating auditor-ready reports on demand
key_moments.speaker: Kevin O'Brien

key_moments.text: Thomas confirms the multi-framework coverage was a specific ask during renewal discussions, validating that the product delivered on a key promise
key_moments.speaker: Thomas Hargrove

key_moments.text: Thomas reflects that Aegis's integrated platform approach was the deciding factor in their vendor selection and confirms it is playing out as expected
key_moments.speaker: Thomas Hargrove

turns.text: Hey Thomas, can you hear us okay?
Yeah, loud and clear — good to hear from you guys, it's been a couple weeks.
Good, good — yeah sorry it took us a bit to get this on the calendar, things have been moving fast on our end with the Comply v2 launch and everything.
Actually that's kind of why we're here today, right, so — Thomas, I also want to introduce Kevin, he's our solutions architect and he's going to be the technical lead on your ISO 27001 prep work.
Hey Thomas, great to meet you — I've been looking forward to this one, Daniel's told me a lot about what Redwood is trying to accomplish this year.

Likewise, yeah, and honestly the timing could not be better because our CISO has been breathing down my neck about ISO 27001 since like January, so — any help getting a clearer picture of where we stand is going to be huge.

Yeah, absolutely, and I think that's exactly where Comply v2 is going to change the game for you — um, before we dive in though, Thomas, can you just give Kevin a quick sense of where you are in the ISO journey? Like, have you done a formal gap assessment yet or are you still kind of in the early stages?

Yeah so we're — we're pretty early if I'm being honest. We had an external consultant come in back in February, they did a high-level gap assessment, and the short version is we have a lot of work to do on the information security management side, particularly around evidence collection and demonstrating continuous monitoring, which as a healthcare organization is like, that's a big deal for us.

Right, and that's such a common pain point, especially in healthcare — the auditors want to see a continuous thread of evidence, not just a snapshot, and that's historically been really painful to pull together manually.
Oh god, yeah, the last time we went through a HIPAA audit my team spent like three weeks just pulling logs and documentation together — it was a nightmare.
Three weeks, yeah — that's — I mean that's not unusual but it's also exactly the kind of thing we built Comply v2 to eliminate, so let's talk about what that actually looks like in practice.
Please, yeah, walk me through it.

So — okay so the big thing with v2, and this just went GA on April 7th so it's very fresh, is the on-demand reporting engine. You can now generate a full ISO 27001 readiness report at any point, and it maps your current control posture directly to the Annex A controls — so you're not having to manually cross-reference anything, the platform does that work for you.
Wait, so it's actually mapping to the specific Annex A controls? Like, is that — is it just a template thing or is it actually pulling live data from our environment?

Live data — so it's pulling from your Detect telemetry, your Identity configurations, your Protect backup logs, and then surfacing all of that against the control framework. So when an auditor asks you to demonstrate, say, A.12.4 on event logging and monitoring, you can literally generate that section of the report on the spot.
Okay that is — yeah that is genuinely impressive, I wasn't expecting that level of granularity.

And it's not just ISO either — you mentioned HIPAA a second ago — the same report engine covers HIPAA, SOC 2, and PCI DSS as well, so if you ever need to spin up a HIPAA evidence package, it's the same workflow, same interface.
Yeah that multi-framework piece is something we specifically asked for in our renewal discussions, so it's great to see that actually shipped — Daniel you might remember that conversation.
I do, yeah, I have the notes from that call — it was definitely on the roadmap and I'm glad we could deliver on it before your audit cycle.

So Thomas, one thing I want to make sure we cover — in terms of your timeline, do you have a target date for the ISO 27001 certification audit? Because that's going to shape how we structure the onboarding to Comply v2.
Our CISO is targeting Q1 2027 for the formal audit, so we have — you know, roughly eight or nine months, which sounds like a lot but when you factor in the remediation work it's going to go fast.

Eight months is actually a really solid runway for this — um, what I'd typically recommend for organizations at your stage is we start with a baseline report run in the first two weeks, just to get a concrete picture of where you are against the framework, and then we build a remediation roadmap from those gaps.
Yeah and that baseline report — is that something Kevin can help us interpret, or is it pretty self-explanatory on the platform?

Both, honestly — the report is designed to be readable by a non-specialist, we have like a traffic light system for control status, but Kevin and I will absolutely walk through it with you line by line on a follow-up call.

Yeah I was going to say — I'd actually love to do a working session with you and maybe whoever on your team is going to be the compliance lead, we can go through the report together and I can explain what each gap actually means in practical terms, not just the framework language.
That would be — yeah that would be incredibly helpful, I have a security analyst, her name is Priya, she's been leading the internal ISO prep work and she would get a lot out of that kind of session.

Perfect, let's make sure Priya is on the next call — I'll send a calendar invite for a technical working session, maybe two weeks out, that gives you time to get Comply v2 activated and run that first report.
That works — and just so I understand the activation piece, is that something I need to loop in my IT team for, or is it more of a — like a configuration thing on the Aegis side?

It's pretty lightweight on your end — since you're already running Detect and Identity, the data connectors are mostly already in place, we just need to enable the Comply v2 module on your tenant and do a quick configuration review to make sure all your data sources are mapped correctly, should take maybe an hour of your team's time.
Oh wow, okay, yeah that's much less overhead than I was anticipating — I was kind of bracing myself for like a big implementation project.
Yeah that's the benefit of being on the full platform — you're not starting from scratch, we're just unlocking capabilities that the underlying data already supports.

You know, I'll be honest — when we were evaluating platforms last year, one of the things that tipped us toward Aegis was exactly that, the idea that everything would be integrated rather than bolted together, and it's good to see that actually playing out in practice.

That means a lot to hear, Thomas — and we want to make sure it keeps playing out that way through the audit, so Kevin's going to be your dedicated point of contact for all things Comply through this ISO process.
Yeah, consider me on speed dial — seriously, if you or Priya hit questions on the platform or you're not sure how to interpret something in the report, just reach out, that's what I'm here for.

Really appreciate that — okay so just to make sure I've got the next steps straight: Kevin sends a calendar invite for the technical working session in two weeks, Daniel gets Comply v2 activated on our tenant, and in the meantime we — or I guess Kevin's team handles the module enablement, and then we run the first baseline report before that working session.
That's exactly it — I'll also send you a short prep document that explains what to expect from the baseline report so you and Priya aren't going in cold.
Perfect — honestly this call was really reassuring, I feel like we actually have a path forward now, which is not how I felt last week.
That's exactly what we want to hear — alright Thomas, we'll let you get back to it, talk soon.
Sounds great, thanks guys — really looking forward to it.

summary_metadata.summary: Lisa Park (CSM) met with Lauren Bishop to discuss the upcoming June renewal, address the March Detect outage, and review the recently released Comply v2. Lauren was candid about internal concerns raised by the six-hour outage and noted that procurement is requiring at least one competitive quote from SentinelShield. The team is planning to expand their cloud workload footprint by about 40 workloads through Q3, which will factor into renewal pricing options. Lisa committed to sending the post-incident review, preparing two renewal proposal options, and scheduling a compliance walkthrough for June.

action_items.text: Send the full post-incident review document for the March Detect outage to Lauren and her team
Prepare two renewal proposal options (base tier and expanded workload growth option) by approximately the 24th
Schedule a compliance specialist walkthrough for Comply v2 PCI DSS coverage in June
Share post-incident review with her team and determine whether a solutions architect follow-up session is needed

key_moments.text: Lauren flags the March Detect outage as a major concern, noting her security team was flying blind for six hours
key_moments.speaker: Lauren Bishop

key_moments.text: Lauren discloses that procurement is requiring a competitive quote from SentinelShield, signaling renewal risk
key_moments.speaker: Lauren Bishop

key_moments.text: Lauren responds positively to Comply v2, noting faster SOC 2 report generation and plans to use multi-framework view for PCI
key_moments.speaker: Lauren Bishop

key_moments.text: Lauren agrees to a compliance specialist walkthrough in June ahead of her Q4 PCI audit cycle
key_moments.speaker: Lauren Bishop

turns.text: Hey Lauren, thanks for making time this morning — I know Q2 planning season gets pretty hectic.
Yeah, no kidding, it's been a lot. But this was already on my calendar so I made sure to protect it.

I appreciate that. So, just to set the agenda for today — I wanted to walk through where things stand on the renewal, get your feedback on how the platform's been performing, and then we can talk about what your roadmap looks like for the rest of the year.
That works. And I do have some things I want to flag, so... yeah, let's just get into it.
Of course. Why don't you go first actually — what's on your mind?
Okay, so the big thing — and I want to be direct here — is the outage in March. The Detect outage. That was... that was not a great experience for us.
Yeah, and I completely understand. I was going to bring that up myself. That was March 10th through roughly the 18th, the event processing issue — six hours of no visibility on threat monitoring.
Right. And look, I get that outages happen, I've been in infrastructure long enough to know nothing is bulletproof, but six hours is a long time. Our security team was basically flying blind.
Completely fair. And I don't want to just gloss over it with a, you know, 'here's what we did to fix it' kind of answer if you have more to say about the impact on your side.

I mean, we didn't have an incident during that window, which is lucky, but it surfaced a gap in our own contingency planning too, so it wasn't all on Aegis. It just... it raised questions internally about the reliability of the platform.

That's really helpful context, and I appreciate you being candid about it. On our end, the root cause was a single point of failure in the event ingestion pipeline — it was a cascading failure from there. Engineering has since deployed redundant processing nodes and implemented a circuit breaker pattern, so the architecture is fundamentally different now in that layer.
Okay. Is there documentation on that? Like, something I can actually share with my team rather than just taking your word for it?
Yes, absolutely. We put out a full post-incident review — I'll send that over after the call. It goes into the root cause analysis, the remediation steps, and the testing we did before re-deploying.
Good. That'll help. My team wants to see that it's not just a band-aid.
Understood. And honestly if there are follow-up questions after your team reviews it, I can also set up time with one of our solution architects to walk through the technical details.
That might be worth doing, yeah. Let me see what the team thinks after they read the doc. Okay, so that's the main concern. What else — you said you wanted to cover the renewal specifically?
Right. So your current contract runs through end of June, so we're about... eleven weeks out. I wanted to start the conversation early so we're not scrambling in June.
Smart. Yeah. What are you looking at — is pricing changing?

So, there is a standard escalator built into the contract — I believe it's three percent — and that would apply. But I also want to understand what your scope looks like going into next year before we lock anything in, because if you're expanding, that changes the conversation.
Mm. Yeah, so we're planning to add about forty more cloud workloads through Q3 as we finish migrating the last of our on-prem stuff. So the footprint is growing.
Okay, that's good to know. Are those workloads going to be on CloudPrime primarily, or is it a mix?
Mix. Mostly CloudPrime, some Azure — we've got about a 70/30 split right now and I think it stays roughly there.

Got it. That's helpful. So I'll put together some options — probably a base renewal at your current tier, and then a growth option that prices in that expanded workload count. I can model out what makes sense financially for both sides.
That'd be useful. And, um, I do want to flag — we're not exclusively evaluating Aegis. I have procurement pushing me to get at least one competitive quote, so... I want to be transparent about that.
I appreciate you being upfront. Who are you looking at, if you don't mind me asking?
Probably SentinelShield, just because they've been pretty aggressive in our space lately. I don't have a strong pull toward them but procurement wants the comparison.
Fair enough. I'll make sure our proposal is competitive on value, not just price. And I'm happy to do a side-by-side if that would be helpful for your internal review.
Maybe. Let me see what SentinelShield comes back with first.
Of course. Just know the offer is there. Okay, I also wanted to touch on Comply v2 — you're currently on Comply, right? The new version just went GA on April 7th.
Oh yeah, I saw the release notes. The on-demand reporting — we've actually already poked around in it a little.
Oh great, any early impressions?

It's... decent. The SOC 2 report generation is noticeably faster than v1. We haven't dug into the HIPAA or ISO stuff yet because that's not really our priority, but the multi-framework view is something we'll probably use for PCI.

That's good to hear on the SOC 2 side — that's been one of the most common things customers come to us about. And yeah, PCI DSS coverage in v2 is pretty robust, so I'd encourage you to test that whenever you get a chance.
We will. Our next PCI audit cycle is Q4 so I want to have that dialed in before then.
Perfect timing then. I can connect you with our compliance specialist if you want a guided walkthrough before you're in the thick of audit prep.
Yeah, actually, that would be useful. Maybe in June or so.

I'll make a note of that. Okay, so to recap what I'm taking away — I'll send the post-incident review for the Detect outage, I'll put together two renewal proposal options with pricing that reflects the expanded workload, and I'll pencil in a compliance walkthrough for sometime in June. Does that cover everything?
I think so. And if we decide to loop in the solutions architect on the Detect stuff, I'll reach out through you.
Absolutely, just ping me and I'll coordinate. What's a good turnaround for the proposal — does two weeks work, so around the 24th?
Yeah, that's fine. I probably won't get to it until the following week anyway, so that timing works.
Perfect. Alright, I'll let you get back to your Q2 planning chaos. Thanks again for the time, Lauren.
Thanks Lisa. Talk soon.

summary_metadata.summary: Raj Kapoor convened a meeting with Tyler, Ravi, Brian, and Mike to prepare for an upcoming SOC 2 Type II audit with a 90-day observation period starting March 1st. The team reviewed key control areas including access control, change management, incident tracking, monitoring, vendor management, and employee security training. Several gaps were identified, including a broken Zendesk-PagerDuty integration, inconsistent hotfix documentation, alert fatigue in the monitoring system, and incomplete security training completions. Action items were assigned to each team member with tight deadlines before the observation window opens.

action_items.text: Complete log retention inventory by Wednesday and build a CI/CD hard gate requiring ticket references for all production deployments before March 1st
Investigate and fix the broken Zendesk-PagerDuty integration and compile a vendor compliance documentation inventory
Pull LMS security training completion report today, follow up with legal on BAAs for vendors touching customer data, and organize access review records
Send calendar invite for alert tuning session (Tuesday or Wednesday) and set up a shared tracker for all action items with a check-in planned for next Friday

key_moments.text: Tyler flags that the hotfix process has been bypassed, with engineers pushing directly to production and backdating documentation — a likely audit finding.
key_moments.speaker: Tyler Washington

key_moments.text: Brian reveals the Zendesk to PagerDuty integration only works 60% of the time, creating a gap in end-to-end incident traceability for auditors.
key_moments.speaker: Brian Cho

key_moments.text: Ravi raises alert fatigue as a risk, noting that unacknowledged alerts could raise red flags with auditors about the effectiveness of threat monitoring.
key_moments.speaker: Ravi Gupta

key_moments.text: Raj summarizes all assigned action items with owners and deadlines, and proposes a Friday check-in with a shared tracker to maintain accountability.
key_moments.speaker: Raj Kapoor

turns.text: Alright, I think we've got everyone on. Let me just do a quick roll call — Tyler, Raj, Ravi, Brian, Mike — okay yeah looks like we're all here.
Yep, I'm here.
Here.
Yeah, good to go.
Here, sorry I'm like thirty seconds late, had a thing.

No worries. Okay so, the reason I pulled everyone together today — as you all know we've got our SOC 2 Type II audit window coming up, and I want to make sure we're not scrambling at the last minute. The auditors are going to want evidence going back several months so we need to be organized now.
Do we have the audit window confirmed? Last I heard it was still tentative.
Yeah, it got confirmed last week. We're looking at a roughly ninety-day observation period, starting March first and running through end of May. Auditors will do their fieldwork in June.
Okay, so we've got like three days before the observation period actually kicks off. That's not a ton of runway.

Right, which is why we're doing this now. I want to go through the key control areas and figure out who owns what. Tyler, I was going to start with you on the access control and change management side.

Sure. So for access control, I think we're in pretty decent shape. We've got access reviews running quarterly, last one was — uh, I want to say January fifteenth? I'd have to double-check the exact date. And we're logging provisioning and deprovisioning events through Aegis Identity.
Are those logs being retained for the right duration? SOC 2 auditors are going to want to see at minimum a year of retention depending on the trust service criteria.
That's actually a good question. Ravi, you're closer to the logging infrastructure — what are we configured to right now?

So right now I believe we're at thirteen months retention for the identity and access logs specifically. I set that up back in Q3. For the broader system logs it's a bit more varied — some are at ninety days, some at a year. I can pull together a full inventory this week if that's helpful.
Yeah, please do that. And flag anything that's under a year, we should probably normalize that before March first if we can.
I can have something by Wednesday.
Perfect. Okay, change management — Tyler, where are we on that?

So we've got the change advisory board process documented, and most production deploys are going through the proper approval workflow in Jira. The one gap I'm a little worried about is the hotfix process — we've had a few cases where engineers pushed directly to prod without a formal ticket and then backdated the documentation.
Yeah, that's going to be a problem. Auditors will look at commit history and deployment logs and they'll see the timing doesn't line up.
It happened, I think, twice in the past quarter. Both times it was legitimate emergencies but still — the process wasn't followed.

We need to tighten that up going forward during the observation period. Can we add a gate in the CI/CD pipeline so that anything touching production requires a ticket reference? Like, a hard gate, not just a reminder.
Yeah that's doable. I can build that out. It'll probably take me a day or two, I'd want to test it in staging first so we don't break the deployment pipeline.

Good. Let's get that in before March first. Brian, I want to pull you in here — from a support perspective, do you have visibility into how incidents are being tracked and escalated? That's going to come up under the availability trust service criteria.

Yeah so we use PagerDuty for alerting and Zendesk for the customer-facing ticket side. I'd say the process is reasonably well documented. The thing I want to flag is that we don't always have great linkage between a customer ticket and the underlying incident record. Like, if a customer reports something and we tie it to a system incident, that connection isn't always formally captured.
Is that a manual process right now or is there supposed to be an integration?
It's supposed to be integrated but honestly it's kind of broken. The Zendesk to PagerDuty link works maybe sixty percent of the time. My team has been working around it manually.
Okay, that's a gap we need to address. Even if the auditors don't specifically flag the integration, if they pull a sample of incidents and can't trace them end to end, that's going to be a finding.
I agree. I've been meaning to get someone to look at that integration for like two months, it just keeps getting deprioritized.
Mike, can you take a look at the Zendesk PagerDuty integration and see what's going on? I don't want to put more on Ravi's plate.

Yeah, I can take that. I'll coordinate with Brian to understand what's actually failing. My guess is it's probably an API auth token that expired or something like that, but I won't know until I look.
That would actually make sense, we rotated some credentials in December.
Yeah, I'll take a look. Should be fixable pretty quickly if that's the case.
Okay good. Let me pivot to monitoring and threat detection. We use Aegis Detect for that internally — Ravi, what's the state of our alerting coverage? Are we logging the right events?

So coverage is pretty good overall. We're monitoring login anomalies, privilege escalations, API rate limit breaches, network egress outliers — basically the standard playbook. One thing I've been meaning to raise is that we have some alert fatigue going on. The tuning hasn't been updated in a while and we're getting a lot of low-signal alerts that people are starting to ignore.
That's actually a risk from an audit perspective too. If auditors see a bunch of unacknowledged alerts in the system that's going to raise questions about whether we're actually reviewing them.
Exactly. I'd like to do a tuning pass before the observation period starts. Maybe a half-day exercise where we go through the alert configs and suppress or recategorize the noisy ones.
Let's schedule that. Who needs to be in that session?
Me, Tyler probably, and it might be worth having Brian there since some of the alerts feed into the support escalation queue.
Works for me. Let's try to do that Tuesday or Wednesday this week.

Okay I'll send a calendar invite. Now — vendor management and third-party risk. This is one that I feel like we always underinvest in until an auditor asks about it. Do we have current SOC 2 reports for our critical vendors?
Uh, I know we have CloudPrime's compliance reports and we have something for Zendesk but it might be out of date. I'd have to check.

Yeah that's something we should inventory. I can take a first pass at it — I'll pull together a list of our critical third-party vendors and check what compliance documentation we have on file versus what's missing.
That would be great. We also need to make sure we have BAAs in place for anything that touches customer data. I know that's more of a legal thing but someone needs to chase it down.
I can loop in legal and make sure those are accounted for. That's probably a quick check.

Good. Okay, let me just summarize what we've got so far in terms of action items. Ravi, you're doing the log retention inventory by Wednesday and the CI/CD gate for production deployments before March first. Mike, you're looking at the Zendesk PagerDuty integration and taking a pass at the vendor compliance documentation. Tyler, you're following up with legal on BAAs and keeping the access review records organized. Brian, you're participating in the alert tuning session. Does that all sound right?
That tracks for me.
Yep.
Good with me.
Same.
One more thing I want to raise before we wrap up — employee security training. Auditors consistently ask about this. Do we have completion records for security awareness training for all staff?
We ran a training in November and I believe we got pretty close to a hundred percent completion but there were a handful of people who didn't finish it. I'm not sure if we ever followed up.

That handful is going to matter to an auditor. If there are any outstanding completions we need to chase those down and document that we followed up, even if we can't get everyone to retroactively complete it.

Agreed. Tyler, can you pull the completion report from our LMS and send it around? We can figure out who still needs to complete it and decide if we want to issue a new training cycle or just document the exceptions.
Yeah, I'll get that today.

Alright. I think we're in reasonable shape but there's clearly work to do before March first. Let's plan to do a quick check-in next Friday to see where things stand. I'll set up a shared tracker so everyone can update their items.
That works. I'll keep an eye on the tracker.
Okay, I think that's everything for today. Thanks everyone, let's get this stuff done.

meeting_metadata.title: Support Case #6977 - Brightpath Commerce Slow Backup Performance

summary_metadata.summary: Kendra Wallace from a retail company contacted Aegis Cloud Security support regarding severely degraded backup performance, with nightly jobs taking 4-6 hours instead of the usual 90 minutes over the past three weeks. David Kim identified a likely root cause: Kendra's environment is running backup agent version 3.4.1, which has a known change block tracking issue that causes over-scanning, while a fix was released in version 3.6.0 in late January. David acknowledged a process gap in that Kendra was not notified about the update. The resolution plan includes upgrading to 3.6.0 and a follow-up call on Monday, with escalation to senior engineering promised if the issue persists.

action_items.text: Send upgrade instructions to Kendra's inbox within 30 minutes of the call
Schedule agent upgrade to version 3.6.0 tonight or tomorrow night outside the backup window
Follow up Monday morning to confirm backup performance improvement and keep the case open
Investigate why Kendra's account was not notified about the known issue in version 3.4.x and escalate to senior engineering and account manager if unresolved

key_moments.text: Kendra describes nightly backup jobs taking 4-6 hours, up from under 90 minutes, lasting three weeks with real operational impact on her retail business
key_moments.speaker: Kendra Wallace

key_moments.text: David identifies throughput has dropped from 400-500 MB/s to 120-140 MB/s and flags that Kendra is running an outdated agent version 3.4.1 with a known over-scanning bug
key_moments.speaker: David Kim

key_moments.text: Kendra expresses frustration that she was not notified about the known issue and the fix already available in version 3.6.0
key_moments.speaker: Kendra Wallace

key_moments.text: David commits to sending upgrade instructions within 30 minutes, following up Monday morning, and escalating to senior engineering if the issue is unresolved
key_moments.speaker: David Kim

turns.text: Hi, is this Kendra? This is David Kim calling from Aegis Cloud Security support, I have case number 6977 pulled up here.
Yes, hi David, thanks for calling back. I've been waiting on this — we submitted that ticket two days ago.

I completely understand, and I apologize for the delay getting back to you. I want to make sure we get this sorted out today. So you're seeing slow backup performance with Aegis Protect — can you walk me through what you're experiencing?

Yeah, so — I mean, it's been terrible honestly. Our nightly backup jobs are taking, uh, somewhere between four and six hours to complete. They used to finish in under ninety minutes. This started maybe three weeks ago, and it's just... it's not getting better.
Four to six hours, okay, that's a significant regression. And this is consistent every night, or are there certain nights where it's worse than others?

It's pretty much every night. I mean, maybe slightly worse on weekends but honestly it's bad across the board. And look, we're a retail company — we have a lot of transaction data, inventory records, customer data — we can't have backup windows bleeding into our morning operations.
Right, yeah, that makes total sense and that's a real operational concern. Um, let me ask — what's the approximate data volume you're backing up each night? Do you have a rough figure?
It's around, uh, two to two and a half terabytes per night. That hasn't changed dramatically. I mean, we've grown a little but nothing that should cause this kind of slowdown.
Got it. And have you made any changes on your end around that time — network configuration, storage infrastructure, anything like that? Or was this kind of just one day it started being slow?
Not that I'm aware of. I mean, I had my team look into it and we didn't push any major infrastructure changes around that time. So we're kind of pointing the finger at your platform, honestly.
I hear you, and I'm not here to deflect blame — I want to find the root cause wherever it is. Let me pull up your account and look at the job logs from the last couple weeks here.
Please do. And while you're at it, I want to flag — we're running incremental backups, not full backups, so the fact that it's taking this long is extra concerning to me.
Yeah, that's a really good point, incremental jobs should absolutely not be taking that long. Okay, I'm in your account now... I can see the job history here. Hmm. Okay so I'm seeing some things.
What are you seeing? Because my team has been asking me for answers and I don't have any.

So I can see that your backup jobs are completing, but the throughput rate has dropped significantly. You're running at roughly 120 to 140 megabytes per second, whereas historically your jobs were running closer to 400 or 500. That's the bottleneck.
Okay so something changed on your end then. Because we didn't touch anything.

It could be a few things — I want to dig into this more before I point fingers anywhere. One thing I'm noticing is that your backup agent version — you're running 3.4.1, and we released 3.6.0 back in late January. There were some performance optimizations in that release, specifically around incremental change detection.
Wait — so you're saying there might be a performance issue in the version we're running and there's already a fix out?

I don't want to say definitively that's the cause yet, but it is worth flagging. There was a known issue in 3.4.x where under certain conditions the change block tracking mechanism could — um — essentially over-scan, meaning it's processing more data than it needs to.
David, that sounds like exactly what's happening. Why weren't we notified about this? This feels like something we should have gotten an email about or... I don't know, something.

That's fair feedback and I'm going to note that in the case. Customers on affected versions should have been flagged for that update and I'll look into why that didn't happen for your account. That's a process gap we need to close.

Yeah, I mean — look, I like Aegis, we've been customers for over a year and overall we've been happy. But this kind of thing is frustrating. We're a retail business, our data pipeline is critical, and three weeks of bad backups is not something I can just shrug off.

Absolutely, and I'm not asking you to shrug it off. Let's focus on getting you fixed first and then I'll make sure the right people on our side hear this feedback. So — upgrading to 3.6.0, that's the immediate step I'd recommend. Can your team do that tonight?
We can probably do it tonight or tomorrow night. What's involved in the upgrade? Is there any downtime, any risk?

It's a rolling upgrade, so there's minimal impact. The agent restarts but the restart window is typically under two minutes. You'd want to do it outside your backup window obviously, but it shouldn't cause any data issues or anything like that.
Okay. And what if we do the upgrade and it's still slow? Then what?

If you're still seeing the issue after the upgrade, the next thing I'd want to look at is your network egress path — specifically whether there's any throttling or rate limiting happening between your environment and our ingestion endpoints. I can also set you up with enhanced logging on the next backup run so we get more granular telemetry.
Okay, that's fine. I mean I'm hopeful the upgrade fixes it but I've been hopeful before with this and it hasn't gotten better on its own.

I get it. I don't want to just give you hope and disappear — here's what I'll do. I'm going to send you the upgrade instructions in writing right after this call, and I'll personally follow up with you on Monday morning to check on how the backup ran. I'll keep this case open until we confirm the performance is back to where it should be.
Monday morning works. But David, if we're still seeing the same numbers Monday, I need escalation. I can't keep going back to my team with 'we're looking into it.'

Completely fair. If the upgrade doesn't resolve it and we don't have a clear answer by Monday, I'll escalate this to our senior engineering team and loop in your account manager. You have my word on that.
Alright. I'll hold you to that. Go ahead and send me those upgrade instructions and I'll get my team moving on it tonight.
Will do. I'll have that in your inbox within the next thirty minutes. And Kendra — again, I'm sorry this dragged on as long as it did. We'll get it resolved.
Appreciate it. Talk Monday.
Talk Monday. Thanks Kendra, take care.
