summary_metadata.summary: Rachel Torres and Maria Santos from Aegis met with Wayne Keller of Atlas to discuss the upcoming end-of-March contract renewal and billing concerns. Wayne's finance team flagged overage charges on the Aegis Detect module for January and February, which were caused by the addition of two new production facilities expanding event volume beyond the 500 million monthly cap. Rachel proposed upgrading to a 750 million event tier at an additional $310/month, which would likely be more cost-effective than continued overages. Wayne also expressed interest in exploring the Aegis Comply module for SOC 2 reporting, and both sides agreed on an annual renewal with a mid-March internal target deadline.

action_items.text: Send a revised pricing summary by end of week comparing the current Detect tier vs. 750M event tier, including Comply module pricing and renewal bundle options
Schedule a follow-up check-in with Wayne after the proposal is sent to address any questions
Share the pricing proposal with finance and legal teams by mid-March to allow processing time before contract expiration

key_moments.text: Wayne raises billing concern — finance team flagged overage charges on two invoices they weren't aware were possible under their current tier
key_moments.speaker: Wayne Keller

key_moments.text: Maria identifies root cause of overages as the addition of Dayton and Phoenix plants in November, providing a clear explanation for volume increase
key_moments.speaker: Maria Santos

key_moments.text: Rachel presents 750M event tier upgrade at $310/month more, noting it would be cheaper overall than continued overage charges
key_moments.speaker: Rachel Torres

key_moments.text: Wayne asks about SOC 2 compliance reporting, signaling potential expansion opportunity with the Aegis Comply add-on module
key_moments.speaker: Wayne Keller

turns.text: Hey Wayne, thanks for making time today — I know your schedule's been pretty packed lately.
Yeah, no, appreciate it. February's always a little hectic on our end, quarter close and all that.
Totally understandable. I've got Maria Santos from our customer success team on the line as well — Maria, you want to say hi?
Hey Wayne, good to meet you. I've been supporting the Atlas account for a few months now so I'm pretty familiar with your setup.
Good to have you both on. So Rachel mentioned this was around the contract renewal and some billing questions — is that still the main agenda?

Yeah, that's exactly right. Your current contract runs through end of March, so we wanted to get ahead of it a bit, talk through the current structure, see if anything needs to be adjusted going into the next term.
Sure, that makes sense. I did want to bring up a few things actually. Our finance team flagged a couple of line items on the last two invoices that they weren't totally clear on.
Okay, yeah, let's dig into that. Do you have the invoice numbers handy or should I pull them up on my end?
I've got them — it's invoice 8847 and 8901. Both from January and February respectively.
Got it, I'm pulling those up right now. Give me just a second.
While Rachel does that, Wayne, can you tell me which line items specifically your finance team was asking about? That'll help us address it faster.
Sure. So the main one is — there's an overage charge on both invoices under the Aegis Detect module. And honestly our team wasn't totally aware that we had a usage cap on that tier.

Okay, I can see it on my end now. So yeah, the overage is tied to event volume — your current contract includes up to 500 million events per month processed through Detect, and it looks like you went over that in both months.
By how much? Because I don't think our operations have changed that dramatically since we signed.
Uh, let me see — January was about 620 million, and February looks like 590 million. So not a huge overage but it does trigger the per-million rate in the contract.
Hmm. And what is that rate?
It's $0.004 per million events over the threshold. So in January that worked out to — roughly $480, and February around $360.
Okay so it's not a massive number, but my finance team's concern is more just the principle of it — they didn't know the cap existed and they want to understand if this is going to keep happening.
That's a totally fair concern and honestly it's something we should have been more proactive about flagging. Maria, do you have visibility into the event volume trending for Atlas?

Yeah I do. So looking at the last six months, Wayne, your event volume has been gradually climbing — which honestly makes sense, you've got more endpoints connected to the platform than you did at contract start. You added, I think, two new production facilities to the monitoring scope back in November?
Yeah, that's right, we brought the Dayton and Phoenix plants online for Detect coverage. I guess that would account for some of it.

Right, exactly. So that's probably the main driver. The good news is it gives us a pretty clear picture of where your volume is heading and we can right-size the contract tier accordingly for the renewal.
What would that look like from a pricing standpoint? Are we talking about just bumping the event cap, or is it a full tier change?

So we have a 750 million event tier that would likely cover your current volume with some headroom. The price difference per month compared to your current tier is — let me check — it comes out to about $310 more per month.
And that would eliminate the overage charges going forward.
Correct. And when you do the math, if you're consistently going over, the tier bump actually costs you less than the overage rate on average. So it's cleaner from a budgeting perspective too.
Yeah that makes sense. I'd want to see that laid out in writing so I can show my finance team, but directionally it seems reasonable.
Absolutely, I'll put together a revised pricing summary that shows the current tier versus the 750 million tier with the cost comparison. I can have that to you by end of week.

That works. The other thing I wanted to ask about — and this is more forward-looking — we've been getting some pressure internally to get better compliance reporting in place. Specifically around SOC 2. Is that something Aegis handles?
Yes, that falls under Aegis Comply — you're currently not subscribed to that module but it's something we can definitely talk through. Maria, do you want to give a quick overview?

Sure. So Aegis Comply is our compliance reporting and audit module. It supports multiple frameworks — SOC 2 is one of them, along with things like HIPAA and PCI DSS. It's designed to give you on-demand reporting rather than having to manually compile audit evidence.
Okay. Is that something that would be priced separately on top of the existing contract?

It is a separate add-on, yeah. We can include the Comply pricing in that summary doc I'm putting together so you have the full picture. Depending on the scope, there are also some bundle discounts available when you're renewing.
Sure, include it. I'm not committing to anything yet but I want to have the numbers in front of me when I talk to our CFO.

Completely understood. So to recap — I'll send over the pricing comparison for the Detect tier upgrade, include the Comply module pricing with renewal bundle options, and we should also talk about the renewal term length. Are you open to a two-year term or would you prefer to stay annual?
Annual for now. We've got some internal budget planning happening and I don't want to lock in a multi-year until we have more visibility into our infrastructure roadmap for next year.
That's totally fine, we can absolutely work on an annual renewal. I'll structure the proposal that way and flag that multi-year options are available if the conversation changes internally.
Sounds good. And timing-wise, if I get this to my finance and legal team by mid-March, is that still enough runway before the contract expires?

Yeah, mid-March gives us enough time to get the paperwork sorted before end of March. I'd say try to target the 13th or 14th internally so we have a buffer. I'll follow up with you if anything looks like it's getting close.
Alright, I think that covers what I needed. Maria, anything else on your end?
Nothing urgent from me — I'll just make sure we have a check-in scheduled after the proposal goes out in case you have questions. I'll reach out to get something on the calendar.
Perfect. Thanks both of you, I'll keep an eye out for that doc.
Thanks Wayne, talk soon.

semantic_chunks.chunk_text:
Rachel Torres: Hey Wayne, thanks for making time today — I know your schedule's been pretty packed lately.
Wayne Keller: Yeah, no, appreciate it. February's always a little hectic on our end, quarter close and all that.
Rachel Torres: Totally understandable. I've got Maria Santos from our customer success team on the line as well — Maria, you want to say hi?
Maria Santos: Hey Wayne, good to meet you. I've been supporting the Atlas account for a few months now so I'm pretty familiar with your setup.
Wayne Keller: Good to have you both on. So Rachel mentioned this was around the contract renewal and some billing questions — is that still the main agenda?
Rachel Torres: Yeah, that's exactly right. Your current contract runs through end of March, so we wanted to get ahead of it a bit, talk through the current structure, see if anything needs to be adjusted going into the next term.
semantic_chunks.chunk_summary: Introduced contract renewal agenda and billing questions.
semantic_chunks.sentiment_reason: Mostly neutral tone with brief positive greeting.

semantic_chunks.chunk_text:
Wayne Keller: Sure, that makes sense. I did want to bring up a few things actually. Our finance team flagged a couple of line items on the last two invoices that they weren't totally clear on.
Rachel Torres: Okay, yeah, let's dig into that. Do you have the invoice numbers handy or should I pull them up on my end?
Wayne Keller: I've got them — it's invoice 8847 and 8901. Both from January and February respectively.
Rachel Torres: Got it, I'm pulling those up right now. Give me just a second.
Maria Santos: While Rachel does that, Wayne, can you tell me which line items specifically your finance team was asking about? That'll help us address it faster.
Wayne Keller: Sure. So the main one is — there's an overage charge on both invoices under the Aegis Detect module. And honestly our team wasn't totally aware that we had a usage cap on that tier.
Rachel Torres: Okay, I can see it on my end now. So yeah, the overage is tied to event volume — your current contract includes up to 500 million events per month processed through Detect, and it looks like you went over that in both months.
Wayne Keller: By how much? Because I don't think our operations have changed that dramatically since we signed.
Rachel Torres: Uh, let me see — January was about 620 million, and February looks like 590 million. So not a huge overage but it does trigger the per-million rate in the contract.
semantic_chunks.chunk_summary: Discussed overage charges on Detect module invoices and usage caps.
semantic_chunks.sentiment_reason: Neutral discussion of overage charges.

semantic_chunks.chunk_text:
Wayne Keller: Hmm. And what is that rate?
Rachel Torres: It's $0.004 per million events over the threshold. So in January that worked out to — roughly $480, and February around $360.
Wayne Keller: Okay so it's not a massive number, but my finance team's concern is more just the principle of it — they didn't know the cap existed and they want to understand if this is going to keep happening.
Rachel Torres: That's a totally fair concern and honestly it's something we should have been more proactive about flagging. Maria, do you have visibility into the event volume trending for Atlas?
Maria Santos: Yeah I do. So looking at the last six months, Wayne, your event volume has been gradually climbing — which honestly makes sense, you've got more endpoints connected to the platform than you did at contract start. You added, I think, two new production facilities to the monitoring scope back in November?
Wayne Keller: Yeah, that's right, we brought the Dayton and Phoenix plants online for Detect coverage. I guess that would account for some of it.
Maria Santos: Right, exactly. So that's probably the main driver. The good news is it gives us a pretty clear picture of where your volume is heading and we can right-size the contract tier accordingly for the renewal.
Wayne Keller: What would that look like from a pricing standpoint? Are we talking about just bumping the event cap, or is it a full tier change?
Rachel Torres: So we have a 750 million event tier that would likely cover your current volume with some headroom. The price difference per month compared to your current tier is — let me check — it comes out to about $310 more per month.
Wayne Keller: And that would eliminate the overage charges going forward.
Rachel Torres: Correct. And when you do the math, if you're consistently going over, the tier bump actually costs you less than the overage rate on average. So it's cleaner from a budgeting perspective too.
Wayne Keller: Yeah that makes sense. I'd want to see that laid out in writing so I can show my finance team, but directionally it seems reasonable.
Rachel Torres: Absolutely, I'll put together a revised pricing summary that shows the current tier versus the 750 million tier with the cost comparison. I can have that to you by end of week.
semantic_chunks.chunk_summary: Explored moving to a higher event tier to eliminate overage charges.
semantic_chunks.sentiment_reason: Neutral technical discussion of tier upgrade.

semantic_chunks.chunk_text:
Wayne Keller: That works. The other thing I wanted to ask about — and this is more forward-looking — we've been getting some pressure internally to get better compliance reporting in place. Specifically around SOC 2. Is that something Aegis handles?
Rachel Torres: Yes, that falls under Aegis Comply — you're currently not subscribed to that module but it's something we can definitely talk through. Maria, do you want to give a quick overview?
Maria Santos: Sure. So Aegis Comply is our compliance reporting and audit module. It supports multiple frameworks — SOC 2 is one of them, along with things like HIPAA and PCI DSS. It's designed to give you on-demand reporting rather than having to manually compile audit evidence.
Wayne Keller: Okay. Is that something that would be priced separately on top of the existing contract?
Rachel Torres: It is a separate add-on, yeah. We can include the Comply pricing in that summary doc I'm putting together so you have the full picture. Depending on the scope, there are also some bundle discounts available when you're renewing.
Wayne Keller: Sure, include it. I'm not committing to anything yet but I want to have the numbers in front of me when I talk to our CFO.
Rachel Torres: Completely understood. So to recap — I'll send over the pricing comparison for the Detect tier upgrade, include the Comply module pricing with renewal bundle options, and we should also talk about the renewal term length. Are you open to a two-year term or would you prefer to stay annual?
semantic_chunks.chunk_summary: Covered SOC 2 compliance module pricing and bundle options.
semantic_chunks.sentiment_reason: Neutral discussion of compliance add‑on and renewal terms.

semantic_chunks.chunk_text:
Wayne Keller: Annual for now. We've got some internal budget planning happening and I don't want to lock in a multi-year until we have more visibility into our infrastructure roadmap for next year.
Rachel Torres: That's totally fine, we can absolutely work on an annual renewal. I'll structure the proposal that way and flag that multi-year options are available if the conversation changes internally.
Wayne Keller: Sounds good. And timing-wise, if I get this to my finance and legal team by mid-March, is that still enough runway before the contract expires?
Rachel Torres: Yeah, mid-March gives us enough time to get the paperwork sorted before end of March. I'd say try to target the 13th or 14th internally so we have a buffer. I'll follow up with you if anything looks like it's getting close.
Wayne Keller: Alright, I think that covers what I needed. Maria, anything else on your end?
Maria Santos: Nothing urgent from me — I'll just make sure we have a check-in scheduled after the proposal goes out in case you have questions. I'll reach out to get something on the calendar.
Wayne Keller: Perfect. Thanks both of you, I'll keep an eye out for that doc.
Rachel Torres: Thanks Wayne, talk soon.
semantic_chunks.chunk_summary: Agreed on annual renewal and timeline for proposal delivery.
semantic_chunks.sentiment_reason: Neutral discussion of renewal timing and next steps.

summary_metadata.summary: Maria Santos met with Brianna Foster and Leo Marquez to address a serious six-hour outage on March 10-11 that caused a loss of visibility in Aegis Detect, breaching their 99.5% uptime SLA. The customer expressed eroded confidence in platform reliability and revealed they are actively evaluating a competitor, SentinelShield, which is offering aggressive pricing and a 99.9% SLA. Additional concerns included platform stagnation, upcoming compliance needs (SOC 2, HIPAA), and budget constraints going into renewal. Maria outlined next steps including a renewal proposal with an SLA credit, pricing adjustments, access to the upcoming Comply v2 product, and an engineering call to address Leo's infrastructure concerns.

action_items.text: Send full technical remediation report and coordinate an engineering call with Aegis engineering team for Leo by early next week
Deliver a renewal proposal by Thursday including SLA credit, revised pricing, and Comply v2 access bundled in
Arrange a demo or early preview of Aegis Comply v2 for Brianna and Leo before renewal closes
Review the renewal proposal and provide feedback with enough time for legal to finalize by first week of April

key_moments.text: Brianna describes the six-hour Aegis Detect outage on March 10-11, citing lost visibility, compliance obligations, and the failure to be proactively notified by Aegis.
key_moments.speaker: Brianna Foster

key_moments.text: Brianna reveals they have been evaluating competitor SentinelShield, which reached out right after the outage and is offering more aggressive pricing and a 99.9% uptime SLA.
key_moments.speaker: Brianna Foster

key_moments.text: Maria discloses that Aegis Comply v2 launching April 7th includes on-demand SOC 2, HIPAA, PCI DSS, and ISO 27001 reporting, directly addressing the customer's upcoming compliance needs.
key_moments.speaker: Maria Santos

key_moments.text: Leo states that the engineering call on infrastructure remediation is the deciding factor for his technical recommendation on whether to stay with Aegis.
key_moments.speaker: Leo Marquez

turns.text: Hi Brianna, thanks for making time today — I know you're probably slammed this week.
Yeah, honestly it's been a rough couple of weeks, but, um, we needed to have this conversation so here we are.
I totally understand, and I appreciate you being direct about that — I'd rather we just get into it than dance around things.
I also pulled in Leo from our security engineering team because honestly he's the one who's been living in your platform day to day and he has, uh, a lot of thoughts.
Hey Leo, good to have you on — I've seen your support tickets come through and I definitely want to make sure we address what you've been seeing.
Yeah, hi Maria. I'll be honest, I've been pretty frustrated. Like, the past week especially has been... not great.
So let's start there — can you walk me through what's been happening from your perspective? I want to hear it directly from you.

Okay so, the outage. March tenth through the eleventh. We had a six-hour window where Aegis Detect just... went dark. No alerts, no visibility, nothing. And we're a tech company, we have compliance obligations, we have customers who depend on us being secure — that's not a minor inconvenience.
And what made it worse honestly was that we didn't even find out from Aegis. We noticed it ourselves because our dashboards just stopped updating. Like, how does that happen?

You're right, and I'm not going to sugarcoat it — that was a significant failure on our part, both the outage itself and the communication lag. We should have been proactively reaching out to you within the first hour.

I mean, we got the postmortem email a few days later, which was fine, but like... six hours is a really long time to be flying blind from a threat monitoring standpoint. Anything could have happened in that window.
And look, I don't want to be overdramatic about it, but it does make us question the reliability of the platform as a whole. If this happened once, what's to say it doesn't happen again?

That's a completely fair question and I want to give you a real answer on that, not just reassurances. The root cause was a single point of failure in the event ingestion pipeline — essentially one component failed and it cascaded. The engineering team has since implemented redundant processing nodes and a circuit breaker pattern, so that specific failure mode is addressed.

Okay but how do we know that? Like, I appreciate the explanation but we're being asked to renew a contract and our confidence in the infrastructure is shaken. Do you have documentation on the remediation? Something we can actually review?

Yes, absolutely — I can get you the full technical remediation report, and I can also set up a call with our engineering team if that would be helpful so you can ask questions directly. I think that would be more valuable than just a document.
That could be useful, yeah. Leo, would you want to do that?

I mean, yeah, I'd want to dig into the architecture changes. But I still think the broader question is around SLAs. Like, what is our actual SLA for Detect uptime and were we compensated for that breach?

So your current contract has a 99.5% uptime SLA for Detect, and the six-hour outage did breach that. I'm going to be honest with you — I've been working with our team on a service credit for the affected period, and I have something concrete to bring to you today.
Okay, I mean, that's something at least. What are we talking?

I want to frame it in the context of the overall renewal conversation rather than just throwing a number out, because I think there's a broader package we should discuss. But yes, there's a meaningful credit on the table.
Okay, fair enough. Let's talk about the renewal then, because there's more to it than just the outage. We've also been, um, we've been doing some evaluations of other options in the market.
I'd expect nothing less, honestly. Can you tell me more about what you've been looking at?

We've been talking to SentinelShield. They reached out to us actually, right around the time the outage happened — which, great timing on their part. And we took the meeting because, you know, we felt like we should at least understand our options.
That makes sense. What's been their pitch to you?

They're aggressive on pricing, I'll say that. And they're making a lot of claims about uptime guarantees — 99.9% SLA on their detection product. Which, after what we just went through, that's an appealing number.
Yeah, and they have a... I don't want to overstate it, but they have a slick demo. We've only done one call so we're early in that conversation. But Brianna, you want to tell her the other stuff?

Right, so, the other thing is we've been feeling like the platform as a whole isn't growing fast enough for where we're headed. We're scaling up, we're going to have some significant compliance requirements in the next twelve months — SOC 2, we're looking at HIPAA as we move into a new vertical — and we need a platform that can grow with us.

Okay, so that's actually a really important thing for me to flag here, because this is directly relevant. Aegis Comply v2 just launched — well, it's going live next month, April seventh — and it has on-demand reporting for SOC 2, HIPAA, PCI DSS, and ISO 27001 all in one place. That's new functionality that directly addresses what you're describing.
Wait, when does that launch? Because we've been on Comply and honestly it's felt a little stagnant. Like the reporting has been clunky.

April seventh is the GA date. I can get you access to the release notes and if you want, we can get you into an early preview or at least a demo of v2 before renewal conversations close. The on-demand piece is a big deal — you're not waiting for scheduled reports, you can pull a SOC 2 report whenever an auditor asks.
That would actually be helpful to see. Bri, that's kind of the thing we were complaining about last month, right? Having to wait for the reporting cycle.
Yeah, it is. I mean, I don't want to get too excited about something that's not live yet — we've been burned before with roadmap promises. But okay, I'd like to see a demo.

Totally fair, and I hear you on the roadmap skepticism — I'll make sure what I show you is real and shipping, not vaporware. So let me make sure I understand everything on the table: the outage and infrastructure trust, the SLA credit, the competitive evaluation with SentinelShield, and the compliance roadmap for the next year. Did I miss anything?
Pricing. Our budget for next year is tighter than it was when we signed. We need the renewal to reflect that reality.
Okay. And where does the renewal land relative to your budget right now — like, are we talking ten percent, twenty percent, is there a number you're working against?

We need to come down meaningfully on the total. I don't want to throw out a number yet because I want to see what you put together first. But SentinelShield is significantly cheaper for what looks like comparable coverage.

I want to make sure we're comparing apples to apples there, because sometimes what looks comparable in a demo is missing pieces — especially around the identity and backup modules which I know you rely on. But I'll build something out. Can I ask what your renewal deadline is internally?
Our contract is up end of April. So we're not in a panic, but we're not that far out either. And we'd want whatever's next decided by, I don't know, first week of April so legal has time.

Okay, that gives us a couple weeks which is tight but workable. Here's what I'm going to do — I'll put together a renewal proposal that includes the SLA credit, a pricing option that reflects your budget situation, and I'll bundle in access to Comply v2. And I'll also coordinate the engineering call for Leo. Does that feel like a reasonable next step?
Yeah, that's reasonable. I'd want to see that by end of this week if possible so we have time to react.
I can commit to Thursday. Leo, any final things from your end that you want to make sure are in scope?

Just the engineering call on the remediation. That's the thing that'll determine for me whether I recommend staying or not. The business case is Brianna's call but technically, I need to feel confident in the infrastructure before I can sign off on anything.

Understood. I'll set that up for early next week. Thank you both — I know this wasn't an easy conversation but I'd rather have it openly and I'm committed to getting you to a place where you feel good about where things are going.
Okay. We'll see what you come back with. Thanks Maria.

summary_metadata.summary: Megan and Chris conducted a launch readiness review for Comply v2, targeting an April 7th release. A recent Detect outage pulled two engineers off the project for four days, causing a 5-6 day slip on the HIPAA and ISO 27001 reporting engine, which has formatting issues in staging. The API is in good shape with beta customers, but customer-facing documentation for HIPAA and ISO 27001 is still in first draft and poses the biggest launch risk. They aligned on key deadlines, escalation paths, and a follow-up check-in for the following Tuesday.

action_items.text: Confirm Priya is fully back on Comply work starting Monday and report go/no-go status on all four frameworks to Megan by the 1st
Send intro connecting Damon to Aisha on the content team for a technical review of the HIPAA documentation today
Raise tiered API rate limiting proposal with the revenue team in Thursday's pricing sync
Ensure Megan is on the distribution list for March 28th load test results and notify her same-day of any failures

key_moments.text: Chris flags that the Detect outage pulled two engineers for four days, causing a 5-6 day slip on HIPAA and ISO 27001 report generation
key_moments.speaker: Chris Lee

key_moments.text: Megan confirms Detect remediation should wrap up by end of week, relieving resource pressure on the Comply team
key_moments.speaker: Megan Lawson

key_moments.text: Discussion of launching without HIPAA and ISO 27001 documentation on day one, which Megan flags as a support and product narrative risk
key_moments.speaker: Megan Lawson

key_moments.text: NorthBridge Financial requesting 100 API calls per day prompts a tiered rate limiting discussion with cost and pricing implications
key_moments.speaker: Chris Lee

turns.text: Hey Chris, you on?
Yeah, yeah I'm here. Sorry, just closing out a Slack thread real quick.
No worries. So, okay, let's just dive in. We've got about — I have a hard stop at two so maybe forty minutes or so.
Yeah same, that works for me.

Okay. So the main thing I wanted to go through today is just the launch readiness checklist for Comply v2. April 7th is, you know, it's coming up fast and I want to make sure we're actually aligned on where things stand.
Yeah, agreed. I think overall we're in decent shape but there are a few things I want to flag, um, before we get too deep into the checklist stuff.
Sure, go ahead.

So the Detect outage last week — and I know that's kind of a separate workstream — but it pulled two of my engineers off Comply work for almost four days. Priya and Damon were both on the incident response rotation.
Right, I was aware of that. How much did that actually set you back? Like in real terms.

It's, uh, it's probably a five to six day slip on the on-demand reporting engine specifically. The SOC 2 and PCI DSS templates are done, those were finished before the outage. But the HIPAA and ISO 27001 report generation — those are still being validated in staging and we've got some edge cases we're still working through.
Okay so the four frameworks are going to be ready, it's just — what exactly is the validation issue with HIPAA and ISO?

It's less a code issue and more a data mapping issue. The way we're pulling control evidence from the customer's environment, for HIPAA specifically, there are like three or four controls where the evidence mapping isn't lining up correctly with the report template. So the output looks weird. It's not wrong necessarily but it's not clean.
When you say not clean, are we talking cosmetic or are we talking inaccurate?

Cosmetic, mostly. The data is correct, the control mapping is correct, it just, um, the way it renders in the PDF export has some formatting gaps and one section where the evidence summary is truncated.
Okay, that's — that's manageable. Can we get that resolved by say March 31st?

That's actually what I wanted to talk through because Priya thinks she can get it done by the 30th, but that's also assuming she doesn't get pulled back into the Detect remediation work. And honestly I don't know what the situation is going to look like next week.

I talked to the Detect team lead on Friday and my understanding is the redundant node work and the circuit breaker implementation should be wrapped up by end of this week. So I don't anticipate any more pulls from your team after that.
Okay, good. That's, uh, that's helpful to know. I'll confirm that with Priya and make sure she's fully back on Comply starting Monday.
Yeah do that. And loop me in if anything changes because I need to know by the 1st at the latest whether we have a go or a no-go situation with any of the four frameworks.
Understood. Okay, what else is on the checklist?

That actually makes sense. I'll take that to the pricing conversation — we have a sync with the revenue team on Thursday so I'll raise it then. But yeah, don't change the default before I talk to them.
Yep, no changes on that until you say so.

Good, make sure I get the results of that as soon as they're available. And if anything fails on that load test I want to know same day, don't wait for a report.
Will do. I'll make sure you're on the distribution for the test results.

Okay, I think we're good. Let's plan to do a quick check-in again next Tuesday, maybe thirty minutes, and by then we should know the state of the Priya work and I should have an update from the content team.
Works for me. I'll send a hold.
Great. Thanks Chris.

meeting_metadata.title: Aegis / Meridian Capital - Service Reliability Discussion

summary_metadata.summary: Suki Nakamura from a financial services firm expressed serious concern over a six-hour threat monitoring outage on Monday that left her security team blind during ongoing regulatory audit conversations. Ravi Gupta acknowledged a known architectural single point of failure in Aegis Detect's event processing pipeline and outlined remediation steps including redundant nodes and a circuit breaker pattern targeted for end-of-month deployment. Suki disclosed that her CISO has initiated a formal vendor review and that the company is actively evaluating competitor SentinelShield. The call concluded with concrete commitments: Ravi will send an infrastructure audit summary by Monday, the technical fix will ship by end of March, and a follow-up check-in call is scheduled for two weeks out.

action_items.text: Send redacted infrastructure audit summary to Suki by end of day Monday
Complete production deployment of circuit breaker fix and redundant processing nodes by end of March
Schedule a follow-up check-in call with Suki in two weeks to review interim monitoring status
Follow up with Suki on Comply compliance narrative features after the Detect issues are resolved

key_moments.text: Suki describes a six-hour monitoring blackout as a serious risk event, noting she had to explain the failure to her CISO during active regulatory audit conversations.
key_moments.speaker: Suki Nakamura

key_moments.text: Suki reveals her CISO has initiated a formal vendor review and that they are actively evaluating SentinelShield following the outage.
key_moments.speaker: Suki Nakamura

key_moments.text: Ravi admits the single point of failure was a known architectural limitation that had not been prioritized, calling it a design gap and taking ownership on behalf of the team.
key_moments.speaker: Ravi Gupta

key_moments.text: Ravi offers to share a redacted full infrastructure audit summary for Suki to bring to her CISO, which Suki responds to positively as substantive evidence she can use internally.
key_moments.speaker: Ravi Gupta

turns.text: Hi Suki, thanks so much for making time for us today, I know your schedule has been pretty packed this week.
Yeah, it has been, honestly. Hi Rachel, hi everyone.
Hi Suki, I'm Lisa Park, I work alongside Rachel on your account — I don't think we've actually spoken before, so just wanted to introduce myself quickly.
Oh right, yes, Rachel mentioned you'd be joining. Nice to meet you Lisa.
And I've also got Ravi Gupta on the line, he's one of our DevOps engineers and he can speak a lot more technically to some of the stuff we're going to cover today.
Hey Suki, good to be here.
Good, okay. So look, I'll be honest with you Rachel, I agreed to this call but I'm — I'm not in a great headspace about things right now, and I think you probably know why.
We do, and that's exactly why we wanted to get on the phone rather than just send another email. I want to give you the space to tell us everything that's on your mind.

Okay. So, you know, Monday was — it was a really bad day for us. We had no visibility into threat monitoring for, what, like six hours? Six hours, Rachel. In financial services, that's not a minor inconvenience, that's a serious risk event.
Completely understood, and I'm not going to minimize that at all.

Our security team was essentially flying blind. We had regulators — we have ongoing audit conversations happening, and I had to get on a call with our CISO on Monday afternoon and explain why our threat monitoring platform was just... not working. That's not a conversation I ever want to have.
Yeah, and Suki, I want to jump in here because I think it's important that you hear directly from me on the technical side of what happened. Can I — is it okay if I walk through it?
Please. Yes. I want to understand what actually happened because the status page updates were... they were not sufficient.

Yeah, and I hear you on that, the communications piece is something we're addressing separately. But on the technical side — what happened was we had a cascading failure in our event processing pipeline. There was essentially a single point of failure in the event ingestion layer, and when that component went down, it took the whole pipeline with it rather than failing gracefully.
Right. So there was no redundancy built in.

That's — yeah, that's the honest answer. There wasn't sufficient redundancy at that layer, and that's a design gap that we've already started addressing. We're implementing redundant processing nodes and we're adding a circuit breaker pattern so that if one node degrades, traffic gets redistributed rather than causing a full outage.
Okay, and when does that actually get shipped? Because I've been in software long enough to know that 'we're implementing' can mean a lot of different things.
That's a completely fair push. Ravi, do you want to give Suki the timeline?
Yeah, so the redundant nodes are already being staged in our dev environment, and we're targeting end of this month for the circuit breaker rollout to production. We're not waiting on this one.
End of March. Okay. And what happens between now and end of March if something goes wrong again?

So in the interim we've actually increased monitoring frequency on that specific pipeline component and we have an on-call rotation specifically watching it. It's not the permanent fix but it's not nothing either.

I appreciate you explaining it, Ravi, I do. But I have to be candid with you all — this isn't just about the technical fix for me. The bigger issue is that this shook my confidence, and frankly my leadership's confidence, in Aegis Detect as a platform we can rely on.
Can you tell us a bit more about what that looks like internally? Like, is this something your leadership is actively asking questions about?

Yes. My CISO has asked me to do a formal review of our threat monitoring vendor options. And I don't love that I'm in that position, because we've been with Aegis for, what, almost two years now, and up until Monday I would have said we were pretty happy. But this... this was bad timing on a lot of levels.

I really appreciate you being so direct with us Suki, that kind of honesty helps us actually address what matters. Can I ask — is the review something that's already in motion, or is it more of a... a contingency your CISO is preparing for?

It's in motion. We've — look, I'll be transparent. We've been talking to SentinelShield. They actually reached out to us a few weeks ago, coincidentally, and we had a demo scheduled that we were going to deprioritize, and now... we're not deprioritizing it.
Okay. Thank you for telling us that.
I'm not saying it to be threatening, I'm saying it because I think you deserve to know where we're at. We haven't made any decisions. But I'd be lying if I said we weren't looking.

No, and I — I genuinely appreciate that, Suki. Look, we're not going to sit here and tell you not to do your due diligence, that would be unreasonable. What I do want to make sure is that you're evaluating us based on a full picture, not just this week.
And that's fair, but the full picture does include this week. That's part of the picture.
Absolutely. No question. And we own that.
I guess what I want to understand from you all is — is this a one-off? Or is this symptomatic of something deeper in how Detect is architected? Because those are two very different stories.

That's exactly the right question and I want to give you a real answer, not a canned one. The honest truth is — the single point of failure that caused this outage was a known architectural limitation that we hadn't yet prioritized fixing. We knew it existed. We underestimated the blast radius. That's on us.
I... okay. I actually appreciate that you said that, Ravi. That's more honest than I expected.

We've done a full audit of similar single points of failure across the Detect infrastructure this week, and the circuit breaker implementation addresses the highest risk ones. I can share that audit summary with you if that would be helpful — obviously redacted where appropriate, but I think seeing the scope of what we've reviewed might give you some confidence.
Yes. Yes I'd want to see that. That's the kind of thing I can actually bring to my CISO — something substantive.
We'll get that to you by end of day Monday, Ravi can you confirm that works on your end?
Yeah, Monday works, I'll have it ready.

Okay. And look, I do want to say — the other piece of this that's been frustrating, and this is maybe more on the compliance side, is that I need to be able to demonstrate to our regulators that we have continuous monitoring in place. When Detect goes down, that argument falls apart. And in our industry, that's not academic.

That's such an important point, and actually — I don't want to pivot too hard here because I know we're focused on Detect today — but we do have some things coming in Comply that might actually help you build a stronger narrative around that for regulators, around your overall monitoring posture.
Maybe, but right now I need Detect to work before I worry about how we report on it.
That's completely fair. You're right, one thing at a time.
So where do we land today? I want to make sure I leave this call with a clear picture of what you're committing to and by when.

Okay, let me summarize and you can tell me if I'm missing anything. Ravi's going to send over the infrastructure audit summary by Monday. We're committing to end-of-month production deployment of the circuit breaker fix and redundant nodes. And I'd like to schedule a follow-up call in two weeks — not to sell you anything, just to check in and make sure the interim monitoring is holding and answer any questions that come up from your internal review.
Two weeks. Okay. Yeah, I can do that. And Rachel — I do want to be clear, I'm not trying to be difficult here. I'm trying to do right by my organization.
I know that. And honestly, the fact that you're still talking to us and being this candid tells me that you haven't given up on us yet, and we're not going to give up on you either.
We'll see. Send me that audit doc and let's talk in two weeks.
Absolutely. Thanks Suki, we'll be in touch Monday.

summary_metadata.summary: The team convened an urgent internal call to address a significant outage of their Detect product that left customers without security monitoring for approximately six hours overnight. A single point of failure in the event ingestion pipeline caused a cascading failure, and critically, the company's own internal alerting also failed because the health checks were tied to the same broken pipeline — meaning a customer, not the team, flagged the outage. At least 47 support tickets have been logged, key enterprise accounts including Fortex and Meridian Financial are expressing dissatisfaction and referencing competitors, and SLA compliance issues are expected to result in credits. The team aligned on immediate action items including drafting a formal executive incident communication, pulling the full SLA exposure list, looping in legal and finance, and preparing customer-facing talking points to clarify that the Comply product was unaffected.

action_items.text: Send confirmed RCA technical details to Diana by noon today
Pull full list of customers with contractual uptime SLAs on Detect from Salesforce and deliver by 4 PM
Loop in legal and finance on SLA exposure and send Diana the Meridian Financial contact information
Draft formal executive incident communication using only confirmed facts and personally reach out to Fortex CISO and Brightline account today

key_moments.text: Aisha reveals that a customer — not the internal team — flagged the outage at 2 AM, triggering the internal escalation
key_moments.speaker: Aisha Johnson

key_moments.text: Diana highlights the critical irony that the health checks used to detect failures were tied to the same pipeline that broke, meaning the monitoring product failed to monitor itself
key_moments.speaker: Diana Reeves

key_moments.text: Aisha flags that Fortex's CISO explicitly mentioned competitor SentinelShield and that they are evaluating their options, signaling serious churn risk
key_moments.speaker: Aisha Johnson

key_moments.text: Priya confirms Comply is on completely separate infrastructure and was unaffected, and will be on track for the April launch — providing a positive data point to communicate to worried customers
key_moments.speaker: Priya Patel

turns.text: Okay, I think everyone's on now — Diana, can you hear us okay?
Yeah, I'm here. Sorry, I was just — I had another call running over. Let's get into it.
No worries. So, um, I wanted to get us all together because the inbound volume from customers this morning has been... it's been a lot.
Yeah, same on the support side. We've had — Priya, you want to give the numbers?

And what did you tell them?
I told them I was getting answers and that I'd follow up by end of day. I didn't want to — I mean, I don't have the technical details yet, I didn't want to say something wrong.
That was the right call, Lisa. Okay, so — does engineering have a root cause yet? Because I am not going to be able to go to these customers without something concrete.

We got a preliminary RCA this morning. The short version is there was a single point of failure in the event ingestion pipeline — basically one component went down and it cascaded. No circuit breaker, no fallback. It just... everything fell over.

Okay so — and I say this with all due respect to the engineering team — how does that happen? How do we ship something into production for a security monitoring product with a single point of failure in the ingestion layer? That's... that's not okay.

I mean, I don't disagree, and I don't want to throw anyone under the bus on this call, but yeah, that's a question that needs to get answered. Apparently the redundancy was on the roadmap but hadn't been prioritized.

No, obviously not. I'm just — I'm giving you the internal context. We need to separate what we're saying internally from what we're communicating externally.
Agreed. Let's focus on what the customer-facing message is. Aisha, you handle Fortex and Brightline — have you talked to them yet?

Wait — a customer flagged the outage to us? Is that confirmed?
That's my understanding, yes. One of our customers noticed their dashboard wasn't updating and opened a ticket around, I think it was 2 AM? And that's what triggered the internal escalation.

This is — okay. This is a significant problem beyond just the outage itself. We have a monitoring product and we didn't monitor ourselves. I need to understand how our internal alerting failed before I can get on a call with any of these customers.

I've already raised that with the engineering lead. They're including it in the RCA. It sounds like the health checks were tied to the same pipeline that failed, so when the pipeline went down, the health checks stopped firing too.
Okay so the thing that was supposed to tell us something was wrong... was the thing that broke. That's — yeah. Okay.
It's bad. I'm not going to sugarcoat it. It's a bad look.
What's the fix timeline? Like when can we tell customers this won't happen again?

Two to three weeks. Okay. Are customers going to accept that?
Some will, some won't. Meridian is already asking about SLAs. Their contract has a 99.9% uptime SLA and six hours down is — that's not compliant. We're probably looking at credits.
Yeah, I figured. Have you looped in legal and finance on the SLA exposure?
Not yet. I wanted to do this call first but that's my next step.
Do that today, please. And I want a full list of every customer who has a contractual uptime SLA on Detect — not just our top accounts, all of them — by end of business. Can someone own that?
I can pull that from Salesforce. I'll have it by 4 PM.
Thank you. Now — communication. We need to get something out. What's gone out so far?

I'll get you what we have. I'll flag that some of it is still being finalized but I can give you the confirmed facts.
That works. Only confirmed facts go in the communication — I don't want us committing to timelines or causes we haven't fully validated.

Good point. Comply was not affected, right? Detect and Comply are on completely separate infrastructure?
Correct, they're fully separate. Comply v2 is still on track for the April launch. The Detect outage had zero impact on that.
Okay good. Make sure your AMs are communicating that clearly. The last thing we need is customers thinking the whole platform is unstable.
Agreed. I'll put together some talking points and get them to Lisa and Aisha before end of day.
That'd be really helpful, thank you Priya. I have like — I have six customer calls this afternoon and I need something concrete I can actually say.
Okay. Let's talk about escalation risk. Aisha, you mentioned Fortex is evaluating alternatives. Are there other accounts we should be worried about from a churn standpoint?

I'd flag Brightline — they're up for renewal in May and the relationship has already been a little rocky. And there's a mid-market account, Kestrel Tech, their CEO is apparently very active on LinkedIn and I wouldn't be surprised if we see something public.

Oh great. That's all we need. Okay — I'm going to personally reach out to the Fortex CISO and the Brightline account today. Lisa, I'll need the contact info for whoever you've been talking to at Meridian.
I'll send it over right after this call.

Alright. Let's wrap up with action items. Priya — RCA details to Diana by noon. Aisha — SLA exposure list by 4 PM. Lisa — loop in legal and finance, send Diana the Meridian contact. Everyone — review and circulate talking points once Priya has them ready. Does that capture it?
Yeah, that covers it on my end.
Same. I'll ping you all if anything escalates before end of day.
Please do. And guys — I know this is a tough day. Let's just stay on top of it and make sure our customers feel like we're being straight with them. That's really all we can do right now.
Agreed. Thanks everyone.

meeting_metadata.title: Support Case #1514 - Meridian Capital Granular Restore Request

summary_metadata.summary: Jason Hoffman from Meridian contacted Aegis support (Sarah Chen) regarding the lack of granular file-level restore capabilities in Aegis Protect. An analyst had accidentally overwritten config files on an EBS-backed EC2 trading platform, requiring a two-hour manual workaround involving spinning up a temporary instance to retrieve the files. Sarah confirmed that file-level restore is not currently supported and documented a formal feature request covering granular file-level restore from EBS snapshots, restore-to-alternate-path functionality, and a file metadata preview capability. The case will be logged, Jason's colleague Marcus Webb will be added, and the CSM will be looped in for follow-up.

action_items.text: Submit formal feature request (Case 1514) for granular file-level restore from EBS snapshots, alternate path restore, and file metadata preview into the Protect product feedback system
Add Marcus Webb to Case 1514 documentation and loop in Meridian's CSM for ongoing updates
Monitor portal for case documentation update expected within the hour
Ensure CSM provides Jason with roadmap updates on the feature request to avoid it going untracked

key_moments.text: Sarah confirms that granular file-level restore is not currently supported in Aegis Protect, validating Jason's suspicion
key_moments.speaker: Sarah Chen

key_moments.text: Jason describes the two-hour manual workaround required to restore a handful of config files, highlighting significant operational pain
key_moments.speaker: Jason Hoffman

key_moments.text: Jason states his trading platform has a 15-minute RTO for file-level restores, making the current workaround operationally unacceptable
key_moments.speaker: Jason Hoffman

key_moments.text: Jason thanks Sarah for being transparent about product limitations rather than making vague promises, expressing appreciation for the honest conversation
key_moments.speaker: Jason Hoffman

turns.text: Hi Jason, thanks for joining — this is Sarah Chen from Aegis support, I'll be handling case number 1514 for you today.
Hey Sarah, yeah thanks for getting on a call, I appreciate it — email threads on this kind of thing just go in circles.

Totally understand, it's a lot easier to just talk through it directly. So I pulled up the case before hopping on — looks like you're looking at granular restore capabilities within Aegis Protect, is that the right starting point?

Yeah, exactly. So the short version is — we had an incident internally, nothing catastrophic, but one of our analysts accidentally overwrote a set of config files in our trading platform environment, and we needed to restore just those specific files, not the whole VM or the whole volume.
Okay, got it. And when you went into Protect to try and do that, what did you run into?

So, the restore options we're seeing are basically — restore the full snapshot, or restore at the volume level. There's no way that I can find to, uh, drill down and say I want this specific directory, or these three files from this snapshot point.

Right, yeah, so that's — that's actually an accurate read of where the product is today. Granular file-level restore, like truly arbitrary path-based restore from within a snapshot, that's not currently a supported workflow in Protect.
Okay. I mean, I kind of suspected that was going to be the answer but I wanted to confirm it rather than assume.
No, absolutely right to ask. So let me just — can I ask a few more questions about the specific scenario so I can make sure I capture this accurately for the feature request ticket?
Sure, go ahead.
So the workload in question — is this an EC2 instance, a VM in a private cloud environment, or something else?
It's EC2, yeah. We're running on CloudPrime, the trading platform environment specifically is in us-east-1. The config files in question are on an EBS volume attached to the instance.
Okay, EBS-backed EC2, us-east-1. And the snapshots are being managed through Protect — so you've got a backup policy applied to that instance?

Correct, we've got an hourly snapshot policy on that environment. So the snapshots exist, they're there, it's just — we can't get to individual files without restoring the whole volume, which is kind of a nuclear option for what we actually need.

Yeah, that makes complete sense. Restoring a full volume just for a handful of config files is a lot of overhead, especially if the instance needs to stay up or there are dependency concerns. In this case, how did you ultimately resolve it — were you able to get the files back another way?

Yeah, we ended up spinning up a temporary EC2 instance, restoring the volume to that, mounting it, pulling the files manually, and then copying them back. It worked but it was, uh, it was probably a two-hour process for something that should have taken ten minutes.

Yeah, that's a pretty painful workaround. I want to be transparent with you — that manual approach is essentially what the product forces today if you need file-level granularity, and I completely acknowledge that's not a great experience.

Right. And look, for a financial services environment, uh, we have pretty strict RTO requirements on certain systems. A two-hour workaround process isn't something I can count on operationally. It kind of worked this time but it's not a repeatable procedure I'd want to rely on.
Understood. And that's exactly the kind of context that's valuable to document. Can I ask — what kind of RTO are you targeting for this class of restore, roughly?

For the trading platform environment specifically, we're targeting fifteen minutes or under for file-level restores. Obviously full system recovery has a different SLA, but for — you know, config corruption or an accidental overwrite scenario — fifteen minutes is kind of our threshold.

Fifteen minutes, got that. And are we talking about, like, structured file types — configs, CSVs, that kind of thing — or do you also need to think about database-level granularity, like individual records or tables?

For now, the ask is really around file-level. Flat files, config files, maybe log archives in some cases. Database granularity is a whole other conversation, we manage that separately through our DBA team with native tooling.

Perfect, that actually simplifies things from a scoping standpoint. So I'm going to log this as a formal feature request — granular file-level restore for EBS-backed workloads, with the ability to browse the snapshot contents and select specific paths or directories for restore. Does that capture what you're looking for?

Yeah, that's the core of it. I'd also add — the ability to restore to an alternate location would be useful. So not necessarily back to the original path, but maybe restore to a staging directory so we can validate before we overwrite anything in production.
Oh, that's a good addition — restore to alternate path, so you can do a staged recovery rather than directly overwriting in place. I'll include that. Anything else that comes to mind?

Um — I mean, the ability to preview the file before restoring it would be nice. Like, just a basic metadata view, see the file size, timestamp, confirm it's the right version before you commit. But I'd call that a nice-to-have, not a blocker.

Got it, I'll flag that as a secondary ask — snapshot file browser with metadata preview. That's actually come up on a couple other accounts so it's good to have it documented with your name attached to it.
Sure. And just so I understand the process — what happens with a feature request like this? Does it just go into a backlog somewhere, or is there some kind of review cycle?

So, the way it works is — I submit this into our product feedback system, it gets triaged by the Protect product team, and they assess it based on request volume, impact, and technical feasibility. High-priority items from enterprise accounts like yours do tend to get escalated for roadmap consideration, but I can't give you a commitment on timeline.
That's fair. I'm not expecting a date, I just want to know it's not going into a black hole.

It won't — you'll have a case number attached to this, and I'll make sure your CSM gets looped in so there's a human who can give you updates on it. Speaking of which, do you want me to copy anyone specific at Meridian on the case documentation?
Yeah, if you could copy Marcus Webb on my team — he's the one who actually went through the whole workaround process, so he'll want visibility into any updates.
Marcus Webb, got it, I'll add him to the case. And just to close the loop — is there anything else on the immediate side that's blocking you, or is this more of a forward-looking ask at this point?
It's forward-looking. The immediate incident is resolved, we're not down or anything. It's more — I don't want to be in that situation again and have the same painful workaround be my only option.

Completely reasonable. Alright, so to summarize — I've got the feature request documented: granular file-level restore from EBS snapshots, restore to alternate path support, and file metadata preview as a secondary ask. Case 1514, I'll get Marcus added, and I'll loop in your CSM for follow-up. Does that cover everything?
Yeah, that covers it. Thanks for being straightforward about where the product is today — I'd rather have an honest answer than get told it's coming soon and then wait a year.

Yeah, I think that's the only way to have a useful conversation. Alright, Jason — I'll get the case updated today and you should see the documentation in your portal within the hour. Thanks for your time.
Sounds good, thanks Sarah.

Jason Hoffman: It's EC2, yeah. We're running on CloudPrime, the trading platform environment specifically is in us-east-1. The config files in question are on an EBS volume attached to the instance.
Sarah Chen: Okay, EBS-backed EC2, us-east-1. And the snapshots are being managed through Protect — so you've got a backup policy applied to that instance?
Jason Hoffman: Correct, we've got an hourly snapshot policy on that environment. So the snapshots exist, they're there, it's just — we can't get to individual files without restoring the whole volume, which is kind of a nuclear option for what we actually need.
Sarah Chen: Yeah, that makes complete sense. Restoring a full volume just for a handful of config files is a lot of overhead, especially if the instance needs to stay up or there are dependency concerns. In this case, how did you ultimately resolve it — were you able to get the files back another way?
semantic_chunks.chunk_summary: Agent explains current restore options and confirms product limitation for file-level restores.
semantic_chunks.sentiment_reason: Agent explains current restore options and confirms product limitation.

semantic_chunks.chunk_text:
Jason Hoffman: Yeah, we ended up spinning up a temporary EC2 instance, restoring the volume to that, mounting it, pulling the files manually, and then copying them back. It worked but it was, uh, it was probably a two-hour process for something that should have taken ten minutes.
Sarah Chen: Yeah, that's a pretty painful workaround. I want to be transparent with you — that manual approach is essentially what the product forces today if you need file-level granularity, and I completely acknowledge that's not a great experience.
Jason Hoffman: Right. And look, for a financial services environment, uh, we have pretty strict RTO requirements on certain systems. A two-hour workaround process isn't something I can count on operationally. It kind of worked this time but it's not a repeatable procedure I'd want to rely on.
Sarah Chen: Understood. And that's exactly the kind of context that's valuable to document. Can I ask — what kind of RTO are you targeting for this class of restore, roughly?
semantic_chunks.chunk_summary: Customer describes painful manual workaround and RTO constraints for file-level restores.
semantic_chunks.sentiment_reason: Customer describes painful manual workaround and RTO constraints.

semantic_chunks.chunk_text:
Jason Hoffman: For the trading platform environment specifically, we're targeting fifteen minutes or under for file-level restores. Obviously full system recovery has a different SLA, but for — you know, config corruption or an accidental overwrite scenario — fifteen minutes is kind of our threshold.
Sarah Chen: Fifteen minutes, got that. And are we talking about, like, structured file types — configs, CSVs, that kind of thing — or do you also need to think about database-level granularity, like individual records or tables?
Jason Hoffman: For now, the ask is really around file-level. Flat files, config files, maybe log archives in some cases. Database granularity is a whole other conversation, we manage that separately through our DBA team with native tooling.
Sarah Chen: Perfect, that actually simplifies things from a scoping standpoint. So I'm going to log this as a formal feature request — granular file-level restore for EBS-backed workloads, with the ability to browse the snapshot contents and select specific paths or directories for restore. Does that capture what you're looking for?
Jason Hoffman: Yeah, that's the core of it. I'd also add — the ability to restore to an alternate location would be useful. So not necessarily back to the original path, but maybe restore to a staging directory so we can validate before we overwrite anything in production.
Sarah Chen: Oh, that's a good addition — restore to alternate path, so you can do a staged recovery rather than directly overwriting in place. I'll include that. Anything else that comes to mind?
Jason Hoffman: Um — I mean, the ability to preview the file before restoring it would be nice. Like, just a basic metadata view, see the file size, timestamp, confirm it's the right version before you commit. But I'd call that a nice-to-have, not a blocker.
Sarah Chen: Got it, I'll flag that as a secondary ask — snapshot file browser with metadata preview. That's actually come up on a couple other accounts so it's good to have it documented with your name attached to it.
semantic_chunks.chunk_summary: Agent documents granular file restore feature request with alternate path and metadata preview.
semantic_chunks.sentiment_reason: Agent documents feature request and scope.

Sarah Chen: So, the way it works is — I submit this into our product feedback system, it gets triaged by the Protect product team, and they assess it based on request volume, impact, and technical feasibility. High-priority items from enterprise accounts like yours do tend to get escalated for roadmap consideration, but I can't give you a commitment on timeline.
Jason Hoffman: That's fair. I'm not expecting a date, I just want to know it's not going into a black hole.
Sarah Chen: It won't — you'll have a case number attached to this, and I'll make sure your CSM gets looped in so there's a human who can give you updates on it. Speaking of which, do you want me to copy anyone specific at Meridian on the case documentation?
Jason Hoffman: Yeah, if you could copy Marcus Webb on my team — he's the one who actually went through the whole workaround process, so he'll want visibility into any updates.
Sarah Chen: Marcus Webb, got it, I'll add him to the case. And just to close the loop — is there anything else on the immediate side that's blocking you, or is this more of a forward-looking ask at this point?
Jason Hoffman: It's forward-looking. The immediate incident is resolved, we're not down or anything. It's more — I don't want to be in that situation again and have the same painful workaround be my only option.
Sarah Chen: Completely reasonable. Jason Hoffman: Yeah, that covers it. Thanks for being straightforward about where the product is today — I'd rather have an honest answer than get told it's coming soon and then wait a year.
Sarah Chen: Yeah, I think that's the only way to have a useful conversation. Alright, Jason — I'll get the case updated today and you should see the documentation in your portal within the hour. Thanks for your time.
Jason Hoffman: Sounds good, thanks Sarah.

summary_metadata.summary: The team convened to address fallout from a six-hour platform outage that caused a complete loss of threat monitoring visibility. Competitors SentinelShield and CyberNova are actively targeting at-risk accounts, including Hartfield Financial and prospect Clearvale Systems. The group aligned on a two-part response strategy: leading with a transparent post-mortem and remediation narrative now, followed by a Comply v2 launch touchpoint in early April to show forward momentum. Key concerns include a potential liability issue involving a customer who claims an actual security incident occurred during the outage window.

action_items.text: Update competitive battlecards this week to reflect the outage response story and SentinelShield's own historical incident data
Prepare a customer-facing technical summary of the incident and remediation steps by end of day Monday
Reach out to Hartfield Financial early next week to schedule a call with their CISO above the procurement level
Prepare a strong follow-up pitch for Clearvale Systems including potential enhanced SLA commitments to salvage the deal

key_moments.text: James Mitchell reveals Hartfield Financial has already been contacted by competitor SentinelShield following the outage
key_moments.speaker: James Mitchell

key_moments.text: Brian Cho discloses a customer is claiming the outage may have delayed their response to an actual security incident, flagging a potential liability issue
key_moments.speaker: Brian Cho

key_moments.text: Tom Bradley outlines concrete remediation steps including redundant processing nodes, circuit breaker pattern, and weekly automated failover testing
key_moments.speaker: Tom Bradley

key_moments.text: James Mitchell suggests pairing the outage recovery narrative with the upcoming Comply v2 launch to give at-risk accounts a forward-looking reason to stay
key_moments.speaker: James Mitchell

turns.text: Okay, I think we've got everyone now — can you all hear me okay?
Yeah, loud and clear.
Good here.
Yep, I'm on.

Alright, so — I want to just dive right in because honestly we don't have a lot of time and there's a lot to cover. The outage last week was bad. I don't think any of us are going to sugarcoat that. Six hours of zero threat monitoring visibility is not something our customers are just going to brush off, and our competitors are absolutely going to use this against us.

Yeah, I've already seen it happening. I had two accounts reach out to me directly asking if we had a formal post-mortem they could share internally, and one of them — Hartfield Financial — mentioned that SentinelShield had already been in contact with their procurement team.
Of course they have. That's — I mean, that's exactly what I'd do too if I were them, but still. It's frustrating.

Same thing on my end. I've got a prospect, Clearvale Systems, mid-market, they were this close to signing — and now their CTO is asking pointed questions about our reliability track record. And I didn't have great answers for him, which is not a position I want to be in.
And what are the competitors actually saying? Like are they sending specific messaging or is it more just, hey, we heard you had some trouble?

From what I can gather, SentinelShield is going in with a pretty polished pitch around their multi-region redundancy story. They're positioning it as, um, you know, 'we were built for this, our architecture doesn't have single points of failure' — which, I mean, honestly Tom, that's landing because it's hitting on exactly what went wrong with us.

Look, I'm not going to sit here and pretend the architecture wasn't a problem. It was. The event ingestion pipeline had a clear single point of failure and it cascaded. We know what happened. We've already deployed the redundant processing nodes and the circuit breaker pattern is in place. But yeah — the damage from a perception standpoint, that's real and it's going to take time.
How long do you think before we can point to, like, meaningful uptime numbers post-fix? Because that's what customers are going to want.

I mean, realistically — we need at least 30, 60 days of clean data before we can say anything with confidence. And even then, some customers are going to want third-party validation, not just us saying we're fine.
That's a long time in a competitive sales cycle.
It is. And Brian, what's the support side looking like right now? How many tickets are still open related to the outage?

So we had — ugh — we had a spike of about 340 tickets come in during and immediately after the outage. We've closed most of them, but there are still about 40 open, mostly from customers who want detailed impact reports, like specifically which alerts they missed during the window, what events weren't processed. And a handful of those customers are, uh, pretty heated. I had one escalation yesterday where the customer's security team is claiming they had an actual incident during the outage window and they're saying our lack of visibility may have delayed their response.
Oh, that's — okay, that's a liability issue. Is legal aware of that one?
Yes, I looped them in this morning. We're handling it carefully.

Good. Okay. So, let's talk competitive positioning because that's really why we're on this call. James, Daniel — what are you hearing beyond SentinelShield? Are VaultEdge or CyberNova showing up in these conversations?

VaultEdge has come up a couple of times but honestly more in the backup and recovery conversation than detect, which makes sense — that's their strength. The Hartfield situation is really squarely about Detect, so it's SentinelShield that's the immediate threat there.

Yeah, and for Clearvale, the CTO actually mentioned CyberNova as well — which surprised me a little. They don't usually show up in the mid-market space at that price point. But apparently CyberNova reached out proactively with some kind of competitive displacement offer. I don't know the specifics but — the timing is not a coincidence.
Of course it's not. They've been watching the news feeds. All of them have. And we would do the same thing — I'm just annoyed we're on the receiving end of it right now.
Can we talk about what our counter-narrative actually is? Because right now I feel like we're reactive and I don't have a clean story to bring back to Clearvale.
That's fair. Tom, can you give me something concrete that James and Daniel can take into customer conversations? Like what's actually different now?

Yeah, so — here's what I can say with confidence. The root cause was identified within a couple of hours of the incident starting. We had the fix scoped within 24 hours. Redundant processing nodes are live, the circuit breaker pattern is deployed, and we've now got automated failover testing on a weekly cadence that we didn't have before. That's real. That's not just spin.

Okay, that's good. That's something. But I think we also need to be honest with customers that this was a significant failure and we're taking it seriously, rather than just trying to gloss over it with technical jargon. Customers can smell a cover-up story from a mile away.

Completely agree with Brian on that. The transparency piece is actually a differentiator if we do it right. Most vendors try to minimize, and if we lead with a really candid post-mortem — like genuinely owning what went wrong — that can actually build trust. It doesn't feel that way right now but it can.

I hope you're right about that, Megan, but I've got to be honest — when I'm sitting across from a CTO who's already been called by two competitors, a transparency narrative is a tough sell in that moment. They want to know it won't happen again, and I can't guarantee that.

Nobody can guarantee that. SentinelShield can't guarantee it either, they've had outages too — it was, what, eighteen months ago they had that authentication service issue? I think it was over four hours.

Yeah, use that. That's a legitimate point — every platform has incidents, the question is how you respond and what you've built to prevent recurrence. I'll work with marketing to get some battlecard updates out this week. The current ones are obviously not reflecting any of this.
The battlecards honestly haven't been updated in like, four months anyway. That's been a frustration for a while. No offense, Megan.
No, you're — you're right, and I own that. That's on us. It's something we've deprioritized and this situation is kind of exposing why that was a mistake.

For what it's worth, one thing that might actually help us in the near term — and this is sort of looking ahead — is the Comply v2 launch coming up. If we can position the Detect recovery story alongside something forward-looking like the compliance reporting upgrades, it at least shows customers we're shipping and investing, not just in damage control mode.
That's actually a smart angle, James. When does Comply v2 go GA, is it still early April?
April 7th is the date, assuming nothing slips. Tom?
We're on track. I don't anticipate any delays on that one. The on-demand SOC 2 and HIPAA reporting features are basically done, we're in final QA.

Okay, good. So let's make sure we're sequencing this right for the accounts that are at risk. We want to get in front of them with the post-mortem and remediation story now, and then have a follow-up touch in early April when Comply v2 drops. That gives us two meaningful reasons to engage rather than just one awkward apology call.
That makes sense. I'll reach out to Hartfield early next week and see if I can get a call with their CISO. I think going above the procurement level is the right move there.
Yeah, do that. And Daniel, for Clearvale — do you think the deal is still salvageable or are we in real trouble there?

Honestly? I think it's 50-50 at this point. The CTO is open to hearing us out but he's clearly been spooked. If we come in with a really strong story and maybe some kind of SLA commitment that's better than what we've offered previously, I think we have a shot. But if we fumble the next conversation, I think we lose it.

Alright. Let's not fumble it. Brian, can you make sure that anyone from sales who needs a detailed technical summary of the incident and the remediation has it by end of day Monday? Something they can actually share with customers, not just internal-facing.
Yeah, I'll coordinate with Tom's team and have something ready. I'd say Monday EOD is doable.

Good. Alright, I think we have a plan — it's not a great situation but we know what we're dealing with. Let's just make sure we're communicating internally as things develop. If anyone hears of more competitive activity from SentinelShield or CyberNova specifically, flag it in Slack immediately, don't wait for the next sync.
Agreed. Thanks everyone.
Thanks, talk soon.
Yep, thanks.
Thanks all.
